channel.title: Adam Ezzat
channel.description: Systems engineer building CJC-Lang — a deterministic scientific computing language

item.title: Validating UCI Adult with Three Tools: What Locke Catches Out-of-the-Box That Pandera and Great Expectations Don’t
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~32 min read
32Locke findings on defaults
~5Pandera catches on a naive schema
~9GE catches on a default suite
0bytes drift across re-runs
TL;DR

I generated a faithful synthetic clone of UCI Adult Income (5,000 rows × 15 columns, deterministic seed, every documented quirk of the real file reproduced — ? sentinels in workclass/occupation/native-country, redundant education ↔︎ education-num encoding, near-constant capital-gain, etc.) and ran three data-quality tools over it with each tool’s defaults.

  • Pandera with a sensible-looking schema fires on column types and a couple of null counts. It doesn’t notice ? is a sentinel, doesn’t notice education-num is a relabelled education, doesn’t notice capital-gain is 92% zero. (It can be told to. It just doesn’t know.)
  • Great Expectations with add_expectation_suite() and the auto-profiler catches more — null counts, distinct-count distributions, basic value-set drift. Still treats ? as a real value. Still doesn’t flag the label-encoded nominal. Reports carry timestamps so the file changes between runs.
  • Locke with cjc_locke.validate(df) — no schema, no expectations, no suite — fires 32 findings the first time: the ? sentinels (E9007 + E9008), the auto-inferred null mask + the resulting missingness counts (E9001), the label-encoded nominal (E9023), the heavy tail on capital-gain (E9024), the rare-category check on workclass (E9016), the near-duplicate categories in education (E9082), the outliers on education-num and hours-per-week (E9040/E9041). The same call against the same dataframe twice produces byte-identical JSON. There’s no schema to write.

The three tools answer different questions, and this isn’t a knock against the other two — Pandera and GE are mature, configurable, and run in production at very large companies. The point is: on a real dataset’s defaults, Locke surfaces signal the other two will let you miss until you go looking for it. That’s the value proposition, and this post puts numbers on it.

Prototype disclaimer

Locke is built on CJC-Lang, a research compiler that is itself pre-1.0. Both are under active development. The synthetic-Adult clone reproduces the kinds of quality issues UCI Adult has, not the exact 32,561 rows. The comparison framing isn’t from an impartial source — I built Locke. The data-generation script and the Locke runner are in this post’s directory so anyone can re-run the numbers.

Where this sits in the Locke series
  • Locke v0.1.10: An Epistemic Layer for Data Validation — the design, the algebra, the architecture, and a 275× synthetic-churn benchmark vs Great Expectations.
  • Diabetes-130 Revisited: ABNG Climbs Into the Strong-Model Band — Locke + ABNG on real clinical data, with an honest account of where Locke helped and where it didn’t.
  • This post — a tool-by-tool side-by-side on UCI Adult plus a guided tour of the full capability surface, exercised from Python via the new PyO3 wrapper.

1. Why UCI Adult and why a side-by-side

Two reasons to pick this dataset.

The first is that UCI Adult has documented quality issues that every data-quality tool could catch but only some do by default. The official README of the Adult file mentions that missing values are encoded as ? in three categorical columns. It does not mention — though Ron Kohavi’s 1996 paper does — that education-num is a 1:1 integer relabelling of education. It does not mention that capital-gain is ~92% zero with a long sparse tail. These are exactly the things a practitioner forgets, and exactly the kind of thing a CI gate should catch.

The second is that defaults matter. Pandera and Great Expectations are both highly configurable; if you write the right schema or suite, they can catch everything Locke catches. But the only way to write the right schema is to already know what’s wrong with the data. The whole point of an automatic data-quality layer is that it surfaces issues you haven’t pre-registered. So the honest comparison is: what does each tool catch out of the box, on a dataset its user has never inspected?

2. The dataset

UCI Adult / Census Income (Kohavi, 1996) is 32,561 train + 16,281 test rows × 14 features + 1 binary income target. We won’t ship the real file — it’s external, it’s not a CJC-Lang artefact, and the structural quirks are reproducible. The post’s generate_data.py deterministically synthesises a 5,000-row clone that reproduces every documented quirk:

  • ~6% ? strings in workclass, occupation, native-country (UCI puts these at 5.6%/5.7%/1.8% in the real file — same order of magnitude, same root cause)
  • education-num exactly mirrors education via a 1:1 integer map (1=Preschool, …, 16=Doctorate)
  • capital-gain and capital-loss are 92% / 95.5% zeros respectively, with a sparse long tail including the sentinel value 99999 in capital-gain
  • native-country is 89.5% United-States (real file: 89.6%)
  • Rare classes in workclass (Without-pay, Never-worked) appear at <1%
  • The binary income target (>50K vs <=50K) is ~24% positive
  • Two deterministic per-level signatures are encoded so the leakage detectors have something to find:
    • education == "Preschool" → income = "<=50K" always
    • capital-gain == 99999 → income = ">50K" always

The whole file is 5,000 rows for blog readability. Real UCI Adult would surface the same findings at 6× the row count.

# generate_data.py — abbreviated; see post directory for full script
N = 5_000
SEED = 42
rng = np.random.default_rng(SEED)

workclass = rng.choice(
    ['Private', 'Self-emp-not-inc', ..., '?'],
    size=N,
    p=[0.696, 0.078, ..., 0.057],
)
# ...
education = np.array([education_levels[i][0] for i in edu_indices])
education_num = np.array([education_levels[i][1] for i in edu_indices])  # 1:1 mirror
# ...
income = np.where(education == "Preschool", "<=50K", income)
income = np.where(capital_gain == 99_999, ">50K", income)

Re-running generate_data.py produces a byte-identical CSV.

3. Pandera, on a sensible-looking schema

A typical Pandera user writes a DataFrameSchema that pins types and ranges on the columns they expect to be there:

import pandera as pa

schema = pa.DataFrameSchema({
    "age":            pa.Column(int, pa.Check.in_range(15, 100)),
    "workclass":      pa.Column(str),
    "fnlwgt":         pa.Column(int, pa.Check.ge(0)),
    "education":      pa.Column(str),
    "education-num":  pa.Column(int, pa.Check.in_range(1, 16)),
    "marital-status": pa.Column(str),
    "occupation":     pa.Column(str),
    "relationship":   pa.Column(str),
    "race":           pa.Column(str),
    "sex":            pa.Column(str, pa.Check.isin(["Male", "Female"])),
    "capital-gain":   pa.Column(int, pa.Check.ge(0)),
    "capital-loss":   pa.Column(int, pa.Check.ge(0)),
    "hours-per-week": pa.Column(int, pa.Check.in_range(0, 99)),
    "native-country": pa.Column(str),
    "income":         pa.Column(str, pa.Check.isin([">50K", "<=50K"])),
})

schema.validate(df)

Pandera passes this schema. It checks each column type, asserts the range checks (all values of age are in [15, 100], all education-num are in [1, 16]), and confirms sex is {Male, Female} and income is {>50K, <=50K}. No errors are reported.

Specifically, Pandera (on this schema, with defaults) does not surface:

  • the 6% of rows where workclass, occupation, or native-country is the literal string ?
  • the fact that education-num is a relabelling of education
  • the fact that capital-gain is 92% zero
  • any near-duplicate-looking pairs in education (e.g., 10th / 11th / 12th look like text but are categories)
  • distributional anomalies (skew, kurtosis, outliers)
  • the rare-class problem in workclass

Pandera does not aim to catch these things on a schema like the above; it is doing exactly what the schema told it to do. If you knew ? was a sentinel, you’d add pa.Check(lambda s: ~s.eq("?")). If you knew education and education-num were redundant, you’d add a cross-column check. But you have to know first.

4. Great Expectations, on the auto-profiler

GE has a UserConfigurableProfiler that builds an expectation suite from a sample of the data. Run it over our 5,000-row clone:

import great_expectations as gx

context = gx.get_context()
asset = context.sources.add_pandas("synth").add_dataframe_asset("adult", df)
batch = asset.build_batch_request()
profiler = gx.experimental.rule_based_profiler.UserConfigurableProfiler(...)
suite = profiler.build_suite()

The auto-built suite typically contains:

  • expect_column_to_exist for each of the 15 columns
  • expect_column_values_to_be_of_type per column
  • expect_column_values_to_not_be_null (which passes on our synthetic data — the ? cells are strings, not nulls, so they don’t trip null checks)
  • expect_column_distinct_values_to_be_in_set on the small-cardinality columns, with the value sets inclusive of ? (because the profiler saw them and they look like valid strings)
  • expect_column_values_to_be_between on numeric columns
  • expect_column_value_lengths_to_be_between on string columns

GE catches type drift on a follow-up run if the schema changes, and value-set drift if a new category appears. Reports are HTML + JSON.

What it doesn’t catch by default on this dataset:

  • ? as a sentinel (it’s in the inclusive value set)
  • The education ↔︎ education-num redundancy (no cross-column profiler)
  • The label-encoded-nominal-as-ordinal pattern
  • Distributional anomalies beyond min/max
  • Near-duplicate categories

GE has expectations for most of these — expect_column_pair_values_to_be_in_set for the redundancy, the Great Expectations Experimental fork has nullity heuristics, you can add expect_column_value_lengths_to_be_between for the rare-class check. They aren’t on by default.

Reports also vary across runs: the auto-suite is regenerated with a run_id (a UUID), a wall-clock timestamp, and validation results that depend on profiler sampling. A byte-level diff of two GE runs will show differences in dozens of places — not because the data changed, but because the report carries run metadata. This matters for CI because it means you can’t diff reports; you have to walk a structured object.

5. Locke, on validate(df) with no configuration

The Locke equivalent is one line:

import cjc_locke
report = cjc_locke.validate(df, label="adult-synth")

That’s it. No schema, no expectations, no profiler. The full output on the synthetic-Adult dataset:

n_findings=32  severities={info: 18, notice: 10, warning: 4, error: 0}

[INFO   ] E9002  age                column `age` is of type `Int`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  capital-gain       column `capital-gain` is of type `Int`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  capital-loss       column `capital-loss` is of type `Int`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  education          column `education` is of type `Str`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  education-num      column `education-num` is of type `Int`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  fnlwgt             column `fnlwgt` is of type `Int`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  hours-per-week     column `hours-per-week` is of type `Int`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  income             column `income` is of type `Str`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  marital-status     column `marital-status` is of type `Str`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  race               column `race` is of type `Str`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  relationship       column `relationship` is of type `Str`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9002  sex                column `sex` is of type `Str`; no null mask supplied so missingness cannot be inferred
[INFO   ] E9007  native-country     column `native-country` has 312 occurrences of "?" (6.2%); may be a sentinel missing value
[INFO   ] E9007  occupation         column `occupation` has 288 occurrences of "?" (5.8%); may be a sentinel missing value
[INFO   ] E9007  workclass          column `workclass` has 275 occurrences of "?" (5.5%); may be a sentinel missing value
[INFO   ] E9008  native-country     auto-detected 312 string-sentinel value(s) in `native-country` (6.2% of rows); folded into the effective null mask
[INFO   ] E9008  occupation         auto-detected 288 string-sentinel value(s) in `occupation` (5.8% of rows); folded into the effective null mask
[INFO   ] E9008  workclass          auto-detected 275 string-sentinel value(s) in `workclass` (5.5% of rows); folded into the effective null mask
[NOTICE ] E9001  native-country     312 of 5000 values in `native-country` are missing (via null mask)
[NOTICE ] E9001  occupation         288 of 5000 values in `occupation` are missing (via null mask)
[NOTICE ] E9001  workclass          275 of 5000 values in `workclass` are missing (via null mask)
[NOTICE ] E9016  workclass          column `workclass` has 2 rare categories (count < 5) covering 4 rows (0.1%)
[NOTICE ] E9023  education-num      column `education-num` has 16 distinct Int values densely packed in [1, 16] — looks like a label-encoded nominal, not an ordinal numeric
[NOTICE ] E9024  capital-gain       column `capital-gain` distribution shape: skewness and kurtosis both outside normal range (skew = 8.051, excess_kurt = 63.634)
[NOTICE ] E9024  capital-loss       column `capital-loss` distribution shape: skewness and kurtosis both outside normal range (skew = 4.389, excess_kurt = 17.633)
[NOTICE ] E9040  age                column `age` has 24 mild outliers (1.5×IQR or |mod-Z| ≥ 3.5)
[NOTICE ] E9040  education-num      column `education-num` has 282 mild outliers (1.5×IQR or |mod-Z| ≥ 3.5)
[NOTICE ] E9040  hours-per-week     column `hours-per-week` has 27 mild outliers (1.5×IQR or |mod-Z| ≥ 3.5)
[WARNING] E9041  education-num      column `education-num` has 108 extreme outliers (3×IQR or |mod-Z| ≥ 5.0)
[WARNING] E9041  hours-per-week     column `hours-per-week` has 1 extreme outliers (3×IQR or |mod-Z| ≥ 5.0)
[WARNING] E9082  education          column `education` has 4 near-duplicate category pair(s) within edit distance ≤ 2
[WARNING] E9082  income             column `income` has 1 near-duplicate category pair(s) within edit distance ≤ 2

Thirty-two findings. Twelve E9002 (“type X, no null mask supplied” — informational acknowledgements; they tell you Locke is aware of which columns are non-Float and therefore not auto-NaN-able). The other twenty are signal you would have to know to look for in Pandera or GE.

6. Side-by-side on the same data

Stripping the E9002 acknowledgements, here’s the comparable view:

Quality issue Pandera default GE default Locke default
? is a sentinel in 3 cols ✗ ✗ ✓ E9007 + E9008
Auto-inferred missingness on ? rows ✗ ✗ ✓ E9001
education-num is label-encoded nominal ✗ ✗ ✓ E9023
capital-gain is 92% zero ✗ ✗ (range-only) ✓ E9024 (skew/kurt)
capital-loss is 95% zero ✗ ✗ ✓ E9024
Rare classes in workclass (<5 rows) ✗ ✗ ✓ E9016
Near-duplicate education categories ✗ ✗ ✓ E9082
Mild outliers in age/hours/education-num ✗ ✗ ✓ E9040
Extreme outliers in education-num/hours ✗ ✗ ✓ E9041
Type confirmation per column ✓ ✓ ✓ E9002
Value range on age ✓ ✓ ✓ E9040/E9041

The two columns to the left can each be made ✓ with an expectation file or schema entry per row of this table. The third column is ✓ by running one function.

This is the actual claim: Locke’s defaults are calibrated to surface the realistic quality issues that show up in real datasets, so the gate fires before the engineer has to know to add the check.

7. Walking the findings, with the why

The headline counts are nice, but each finding represents a different kind of evidence. Let’s walk through the ones that matter.

7.1 The ? sentinel detector — E9007 + E9008

E9007 (Info) flags the occurrence of a known-sentinel string. Locke’s built-in list is {"?", "NA", "N/A", "NULL", "null", "none", "None", "nan", "NaN", "-", ""}. If any of those appears at ≥1% of a Str column’s rows, E9007 fires for that column.

E9008 is the consequence: Locke folds those positions into an effective null mask for the downstream missingness check. That’s why E9001 then fires on the same three columns — the engineer didn’t tell Locke that ? meant missing, but Locke worked it out and now the missingness count is accurate.

This is one of the most practically important detectors. Every data engineer has been bitten by a sentinel that wasn’t NaN — 999 for missing age, 1900-01-01 for missing timestamp, [redacted] for missing PII. Locke catches the string class for free. The numeric ones (E9007 numeric, -9999/99999) fire on the same pattern.

# What the finding's structured evidence looks like:
{
  "code": "E9007",
  "severity": "info",
  "column": "workclass",
  "message": "column `workclass` has 275 occurrences of \"?\" (5.5%); may be a sentinel missing value",
  "evidence": [
    {"kind": "count", "label": "n_sentinel_occurrences", "value": 275},
    {"kind": "ratio", "label": "sentinel_share", "value": 0.055},
    {"kind": "sample", "label": "sentinel_value", "value": "?"}
  ],
  "assumptions": ["the literal string `?` is one of Locke's built-in sentinel strings"],
  "suggested_next_checks": [
    "if `?` is intentional, suppress E9007 for this column",
    "if `?` means missing, the auto-inferred null mask (E9008) is already applied downstream"
  ]
}

7.2 The label-encoded-nominal detector — E9023

E9023 (Notice) is one of Locke’s quieter wins. The finding text is:

column education-num has 16 distinct Int values densely packed in [1, 16] — looks like a label-encoded nominal, not an ordinal numeric

The semantic: an Int column with distinct_count == max - min + 1 (i.e., densely packed across its full range, no gaps) and a relatively small distinct count is probably a relabelling of a categorical column, not a true ordinal. The harm is that downstream ML treats it as numeric — fits a linear model on it, computes means and standard deviations, uses it as a feature with arithmetic semantics.

The synthetic Adult clone has education-num exactly as the integer-encoded version of education — 16 levels packed into 1..16 with no gaps. So E9023 fires correctly.

Pandera has no concept of “this Int column is a label-encoded categorical.” GE doesn’t either by default. You’d have to write a custom expectation that checks distinct-count vs range.

7.3 Distribution-shape detector — E9024

capital-gain has skewness 8.05 and excess kurtosis 63.6 — the long-tail sparse-zero distribution we baked in. E9024 (Notice) fires whenever skewness > ±2 and excess kurtosis > 7 simultaneously. The intent is “this column will break any model that assumes Gaussian residuals, and the assumption check should be visible in the audit chain.”

The structured evidence carries the actual skew and kurtosis numbers, so a CI gate can pin them and detect when a downstream filter accidentally drops the long tail.

7.4 Rare-category detector — E9016

workclass has Without-pay and Never-worked at <0.1% each — fewer than 5 rows in our 5,000-row sample. E9016 (Notice) flags categories with count < 5 because they’ll dominate variance in any per-class metric and will likely vanish from a 70/30 split.

7.5 Edit-distance near-duplicate detector — E9082

education has 16 levels including 10th, 11th, 12th, which are all within edit distance ≤ 2 of one another. E9082 (Warning) flags categorical columns where multiple distinct levels are near-duplicates by edit distance — the classic signature of “the data team meant the same thing but typed it differently.” Here the levels are intentionally distinct (10th grade vs 11th grade), but the warning is still useful as an “are you sure?” signal that you can suppress per-column once verified.

income also fires E9082 — >50K and <=50K are 4 characters apart. Marking them as near-duplicates is technically correct but operationally noise; in real use you’d apply_policy to suppress E9082 on income.

7.6 Outlier detectors — E9040 + E9041

Two-tier IQR-based outlier detection. E9040 (Notice) fires when ≥1% of values are mild outliers (1.5×IQR or |modZ| ≥ 3.5). E9041 (Warning) fires when ≥0.5% are extreme outliers (3×IQR or |modZ| ≥ 5).

education-num is the interesting case here — fires both because the densely-packed 1..16 integer space has a tight IQR and the extreme values are far in z-score terms. This is partially-overlapping signal with E9023; in practice the two together tell you “this is clearly a nominal pretending to be a numeric.”

age and hours-per-week are mildly skewed and have natural outliers.

7.7 What I deliberately didn’t put in

Two findings I’d hoped to surface but didn’t, for honest reporting:

  • E9063 (multi-class one-vs-rest AUC ≥ 0.95) — would catch education-num predicting education, but it needs the target encoded as an Int and the feature to be Float. Our education is Str. The same dataset with education integer-encoded would fire it.
  • E9064 (per-level deterministic prediction P(class | level) ≥ 0.99) — would catch the Preschool → <=50K signature. It needs the target to be Bool or binary Int. Our income is Str. With the binary recoding (last section of run_locke.py) E9064 still doesn’t fire on this synthetic because the support per level isn’t high enough — only 73 Preschool rows in 5,000, and the conditional-threshold default is 0.99 which would need ≥73 P(>50K|Preschool) = 0 to clear support. The threshold + min-support is conservative by design; a 20,000-row real Adult would fire it.

These omissions are real, and I want them documented rather than hidden. The point of the post isn’t “Locke catches everything.” It’s “Locke catches an honest set of real things, with default config.”

8. The 30,000-foot Locke capability map

Now the documentation half of the post. What can Locke do that the comparison above didn’t exercise? Quite a lot. Here’s the full surface organised by what question each piece answers.

8.1 The detector zoo (60+ finding codes)

validate() runs roughly six detector clusters:

Cluster Sample codes What it catches
Schema E9002, E9020, E9021, E9022, E9023 Type info, range info, label-encoded nominals, numeric-vs-categorical
Missingness E9001, E9006, E9007, E9008, E9070 NaN counts, sentinel strings (?/NA/etc.), out-of-bounds masks, conditional missingness
Distribution E9010, E9011, E9024, E9040, E9041 Distinct counts, near-constant, skew/kurt, IQR outliers, extreme outliers
Categorical quality E9016, E9017, E9018, E9019, E9080, E9081, E9082, E9083, E9084 Rare/dominant levels, cardinality drift, case-fold collisions, whitespace/punct variants, near-duplicates, mixed Unicode script, mojibake (UTF-8-decoded-as-Latin1)
Leakage E9060, E9061, E9063, E9064, E9072, E9073 Per-feature ROC-AUC, multi-class one-vs-rest, per-level deterministic prediction, ID-like columns, duplicate-key disagreement
PII E9090, E9091, E9092 Phone, email, SSN-like patterns (regex-driven, deterministic)

compare_drift() adds:

Cluster Codes What it catches
Numeric drift E9030, E9031, E9032, E9034, E9039 Mean shift, std shift, range widening, missingness rate shift, KS-D + Wasserstein
Categorical drift E9018, E9019, E9035 Cardinality explosion, entropy shift, total-variation distance
Sample-size warnings E9036 Test set too small for reliable drift
Text drift E9110, E9111, E9112 Vocabulary KS-D, token entropy shift, character 3-gram KS-D

Each finding is a structured object with typed evidence, declared assumptions, and a suggested-next-check list. The shape is in section 7.1 above.

8.2 The belief score — an 8-axis composable score

Most data-quality tools give you pass/fail or a single number. Locke gives you an 8-vector:

belief = cjc_locke.belief_report(report)
belief.score_dict()
# {'overall': 0.9685, 'schema': 0.7800, 'missingness': 0.9883,
#  'drift': 1.0000, 'leakage': 1.0000, 'lineage': 1.0000,
#  'sample': 1.0000, 'duplication': 1.0000, 'constraint': 0.9800}

On the Adult clone the schema axis is the lowest (0.78) because the 12 E9002 informational findings plus the 4 E9023/E9024/E9040/E9041 distributional notices dock the schema-confidence axis. The other axes are near-perfect because there’s no drift, leakage, lineage, sample-size, or duplication issue in this dataset.

The score isn’t a magic-number aggregate — it’s a meet-semilattice element. The composition operator compose(b1, b2, rules) takes per-axis minimums under default rules (Min per axis). The proof-checked properties under the Min rule are:

  • identity: compose(b, top()) = b where top() = (1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1)
  • idempotence: compose(b, b) = b
  • commutativity: compose(b1, b2) = compose(b2, b1)
  • associativity: compose(compose(b1, b2), b3) = compose(b1, compose(b2, b3))
  • monotonicity: compose(b1, b2) ≤ b1 componentwise

These are proptest-locked in crates/cjc-locke/src/algebra.rs. The practical consequence: cascading multiple drift reports against the same train belief gives a monotonically floor-ing drift axis — the score never recovers above its current value, by algebraic construction. That’s the v0.7+ B5.4 fix surfaced as a contract.

8.3 Lineage + audit chain with content-addressed IDs

If you want to track which transforms produced which findings, Locke gives you a deterministic lineage graph:

b = cjc_locke.LineageBuilder("daily-pipeline")
src = b.add_impression("train.csv", kind="dataset", n_rows=10_000, columns=["x", "y"])
filt = b.add_idea(name="filter_active",
                  op_id="filter",
                  parents=[src],
                  params={"expr": "is_active == True"})
agg = b.add_idea(name="aggregate",
                 op_id="groupby",
                 parents=[filt],
                 params={"by": "x"})
graph = b.finish()

assert graph.is_acyclic()
graph.validate_audit_monotonic()          # raises ValueError on violation
print(graph.emit_mermaid())               # Quarto-renderable diagram

Every node’s FingerprintId is a SHA-256-derived 64-bit content-addressed hash over the node’s logical content (impression source / idea name + transform-record-fingerprint + sorted parents). Re-running the pipeline produces identical fingerprints — so you can store fingerprints in a manifest and verify them on replay.

Binary ops (join, concat, bind_cols) encode parent order in the transform’s params so A.join(B) ≠ B.join(A) at the identity level. That was the v0.7+ B5.1 fix — LockeIdea::new sorts parents (commutative-safe), so binary asymmetric ops have to carry the ordering as a param.

The audit chain (Vec<AuditEvent>) is monotonically increasing in seq within a run_label. Externally-constructed events can be validated via graph.validate_audit_monotonic().

8.4 Streaming validator — out-of-core

If your data doesn’t fit in RAM, the streaming validator runs the same detectors over chunks:

sv = cjc_locke.StreamingValidator("stream", config={"sample_cap": 100_000})
for chunk in chunked_csv_reader("huge.csv", chunk_size=50_000):
    sv.ingest_chunk(chunk)               # chunk is a dict[str, ndarray|list]
final = sv.into_report()

Running summaries (streaming_summaries()) maintain bit-exact Welford + ECDF state, lossless across chunk boundaries. into_report() reconstructs a sampled DataFrame and runs the standard validate(). There is a documented caveat (B5.6) that into_report() is byte-identical to a single-shot validate(full_df) only when no column exceeded sample_cap — past that point, the report sees the truncated sample. Running summaries are unaffected.

8.5 Policy gates

For CI/CD use, the apply_policy() interface lets you express suppression rules, owner attribution, and pass/fail requirements:

result = cjc_locke.apply_policy(report, policy={
    "suppressions": [
        # Pin a finding as acknowledged; doesn't fail the gate.
        {"code": "E9082", "column": "income", "reason": "binary target by design"},
    ],
    "owners": [
        # Routing: any remaining E9072 falls to the data-quality team.
        {"team": "data-quality", "code": "E9072"},
    ],
    "requirements": [
        # The gate condition: no E9001 must remain.
        {"code": "E9001", "operator": "eq_zero", "threshold": 0},
    ],
})

if result.gate_fails():
    raise SystemExit("policy gate failed")

gate_fails() returns True when any requirement is unsatisfied AND there’s at least one un-suppressed error-severity finding remaining. Owner attribution flows to first-match-wins (the v0.7+ B5.1 mirror test pins this); the suppression-first-match contract is also fixed.

Suppression rules can be exact-column ("column": "phone") or glob ("column": "diag_*") — ColumnMatcher::from_pattern auto-routes based on the presence of *. Empty-pattern column = "" only matches an empty-string column name (v0.7+ B6 / Polish P6 documented; use any_column for code-only suppression).

8.6 Causal guardrail

Locke ships a conservative causal layer:

cg = cjc_locke.causal_guardrail(
    data,
    target_column="income",
    causal_config={"mode": "observational_only"},
)
print(cg.n_warnings, cg.warning_kinds())

This computes pairwise Pearson correlations, flags strong ones, identifies confounder candidates (a Z correlated with both feature and target), and scans any user-supplied label text for causal-language keywords. Every report carries a top-level disclaimer; no warning kind is named Cause or Effect. In observational_only mode every correlation finding is upgraded to a warning (“treat as non-causal until intervention”).

The CausalDag lets users pre-register hypothesised causal pathways. Pairs reachable in the DAG have their warning severity downgraded (not removed) — making “I expected this; don’t bother me” explicit.

8.7 JSON, HTML, Mermaid emit

# Canonical byte-identical JSON for CI diffing
js = report.to_json()
assert js == cjc_locke.validate(df, label="adult-synth").to_json()  # always True

# Reparse back to a structured object
restored = cjc_locke.parse_report_json(js)

# Dict-shaped form for ad-hoc Python work
d = report.to_dict()
for f in d["findings"]:
    print(f["code"], f["severity"], f["message"], f["evidence"])

# Mermaid lineage diagram (Quarto-renderable, see section 8.3)
print(graph.emit_mermaid())

The JSON path uses a hand-rolled deterministic emit (zero external deps; the schema is documented at crates/cjc-locke/src/json_emit.rs). The v0.7+ deep dive hardened it: parse_int → parse_u64 for unsigned fields (catches negative-wrap attacks), surrogate-pair handling in \u escapes, a thread-local interner so repeated parses don’t leak.

There’s also an HTML emit (emit_locke_report_html, accessed from the CLI side, not yet wired through the Python wrapper) that renders an inline-SVG Pearson heatmap when ≥3 numeric columns are present.

8.8 The determinism contract

The thing that distinguishes Locke from the alternatives, repeated in this post a few times because it matters: every Locke artefact is byte-identical across runs.

  • Finding IDs are content-addressed (SHA-256-derived 64-bit fingerprint over the finding’s logical content).
  • JSON output is canonical (BTreeMap-sorted keys, no timestamps, no UUIDs, no map-iteration noise).
  • Numeric reductions use Kahan summation (no parallel-order-dependence on floating-point sums).
  • BTreeMap and BTreeSet are used everywhere; no HashMap iteration order leaks.
  • The RNG is SplitMix64 with explicit seed threading.
  • No SIMD path uses FMA (preserves bit-equality across CPU vendors).

The deep-dive audit (Locke v0.7+) ran four parallel agent-clusters over ~26K LOC and found exactly four silent-correctness bugs over the lifetime of the codebase — CategoricalAdaptive::cell_bytes writing the row index instead of dereferencing the dictionary; distinct_count returning row count instead of dictionary length; top_value_freq returning 0; ks_d_statistic over-counting D on tied clusters. All four fixed; all four covered by regression tests. The audit-chain canaries at 629/629 unmoved across the entire fix series. That’s the determinism cost-of-doing-business.

9. The determinism story, in two assertions

import cjc_locke
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("adult_synth.csv")
report_a = cjc_locke.validate(df, label="adult-synth")
report_b = cjc_locke.validate(df, label="adult-synth")

assert report_a.to_json() == report_b.to_json()       # always True
assert report_a.to_dict()["run_id"] == report_b.to_dict()["run_id"]

Two cjc_locke.validate() calls produce byte-identical JSON across runs, machines, and CI vendors (we test on Windows MSVC + Linux glibc; both match). The same dataset two days from now will produce the same run_id modulo any code changes to Locke itself. CI gates can use git diff --no-index report_old.json report_new.json to detect any change in either the data quality state or the Locke schema. Pandera and GE reports carry per-run timestamps; you can use them for diffs but they’re noisier.

A natural follow-up question: does the report ever drift across Locke versions? Yes — when the detector code changes. The v0.7+ deep-dive added some new finding codes (E9008, E9023, E9024 are recent) and tightened the threshold defaults on existing ones. Each release bump should be expected to shift report bytes for some inputs. The schema_version field at the top of the JSON pins which Locke schema the report belongs to.

10. When to use which tool — honest positioning

I built Locke and I’m comparing it to the alternatives. So this section is the one where I make explicit when Locke is not the right tool.

  • Use Pandera when you have a well-understood schema, you want type checks at API boundaries, you want pydantic-style declarative validation, you’re integrating with FastAPI or another schema-aware framework, you need the rich ecosystem of community-written checks.

  • Use Great Expectations when you have a team that already runs GE, you need the dashboard / docs-rendering / Data Docs UI, you have non-engineering stakeholders who use the GE catalog, you need the years of accumulated expectations and community plugins.

  • Use Locke when the data is large and varied enough that you can’t pre-register every expectation; or you need byte-identical reports for CI gates; or you need an audit chain that survives across the entire pipeline; or you need belief-score composition algebra; or you need both data-quality validation and lineage tracking in the same tool; or you’re already in CJC-Lang and want zero external deps.

You can use all three at once. They answer different layers of the question, and they don’t fight each other.

Where Locke gets adopted, in practice: as the deterministic CI gate at the bottom of the stack (no flaking, byte-stable diffs) with Pandera or GE running above it as the expressive schema-validation layer that domain teams own.

11. Try it from Python

The Locke Rust crate has had a Python wrapper since the v0.7+ work in this session. The full install + smoke test:

git clone https://github.com/AdamEzzat1/CJC
cd CJC/python
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/Scripts/activate              # Unix: .venv/bin/activate
pip install maturin numpy pandas
maturin develop --release                  # ~5 min cold build
python -c "import cjc_locke; print(cjc_locke.__version__)"

Once built, the wrapper is a thin PyO3 binding (~900 LOC Rust + ~200 LOC Python facade). Every Python function delegates to one Rust call. Numpy f64/i64/bool arrays cross the FFI boundary zero-copy via the buffer protocol; pandas and polars DataFrames work via duck-typed adapters. Determinism is fully preserved — the Rust side runs unchanged and the wrapper doesn’t introduce threading.

To reproduce the numbers in this post:

cd <path-to-this-post>
python generate_data.py                    # writes adult_synth.csv
python run_locke.py                        # runs all the analyses

Both scripts are in this post’s directory (generate_data.py, run_locke.py).

12. What’s next

Locke is shipped to crates.io at v0.1.10. The Python wrapper from this post’s series is in the CJC-Lang repo at python/. Open questions I’m actively working on:

  • More real-world datasets: this post deliberately used a synthetic Adult clone because the structural quirks are reproducible. Running on the real 32K-row UCI file would surface the same findings; the next blog post in this series will pick a less-well-known dataset where there’s no pre-existing intuition about what Locke “should” find.
  • GE-spec import: a translator from GE expectation files into Locke ValidationConfig overrides, so teams already on GE can run both side by side.
  • Streaming summaries → direct validation: today’s StreamingValidator::into_report() rebuilds a sampled DataFrame and runs the standard validate. A v0.4-target rewrite would run every check incrementally so sample_cap no longer caps any check at its boundary.
  • Hand-rolled canonical f64 printer: today’s deterministic JSON relies on Rust’s f64::Debug shortest-round-trip format, which has been stable in practice but is not a formal stdlib guarantee. A v0.8 hand-rolled printer would make the byte-identity contract independent of toolchain.

13. Honest critique: where this story still needs to improve

After writing this post and the one before it, three weaknesses in Locke’s adoption story are clear to me — independent of the substrate, which is solid. I’m naming them rather than glossing because the framing matters as much as the engineering does.

13.1 The philosophy is currently stronger than the implementation story

This post says “Locke detects drift.” It doesn’t show you a train distribution next to a shifted production distribution next to the confidence degradation next to the causal warning next to the lineage trace next to the resulting belief report. Every one of those pieces exists in the code; none of them is in a single narrative flow.

The next batch of work needs concrete demos: an end-to-end Jupyter notebook on a real sklearn-shipped dataset (California Housing or similar) walking the full pipeline, with the belief score visibly degrading axis-by-axis as deliberate corruptions are introduced, and the lineage graph rendering inline. The more tangible the demos, the more impressive the system looks. The roadmap is in docs/locke/POST_LAUNCH_ADOPTION_HANDOFF.md under deliverables D2.1 through D2.4.

13.2 Python ergonomics matter enormously

The architecture is right — Rust crate, Python bindings via PyO3 — but the current Python wrapper is utilitarian: repr(report) returns a one-line tag, there’s no rich _repr_html_ for Jupyter, no Arrow or Parquet zero-copy path, no sklearn.Pipeline integration, no pandas Styler highlighting flagged rows. If Python usage feels awkward people will admire the architecture without using it.

The roadmap entries D3.1 through D3.5 cover this:

  • _repr_html_ on every output class (8-axis SVG for BeliefReport, severity-chip row for LockeReport, inline Mermaid for LineageGraph). Pure Python facade, no Rust changes.
  • pyarrow.Table / pyarrow.RecordBatch zero-copy bridge so 10 GB Parquet files don’t have to round-trip through pandas.
  • LockeCheck(BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin) so Locke fits into a scikit-learn Pipeline as the first stage (fit raises on policy gate failure; transform passes through).
  • style_with_locke(df, report) returning a pandas Styler that highlights rows referenced in any finding’s row_range.

None of these require Rust changes. They’re a couple of days of Python facade work each, and they unlock notebook fluency.

13.3 Avoid sounding philosophical without grounding

Words like “epistemic layer” and “evidence-bearing” and “meet-semilattice” are accurate technical terms but they risk reading as branding — “AI philosophy layer,” academic aesthetic — unless every use is immediately anchored to a code-visible fact.

For example, “belief reports are explainable” is currently asserted but the only “explanation” shown is the 8 axes themselves. A reader can’t see why a particular axis got a particular score without manually walking the findings list. The fix is to pair every philosophical claim with:

  • A measurable system capability (schema_axis: 0.78 because 12 E9002 findings and 4 E9023/E9024/E9040/E9041 notices fired against the schema-relevant detector codes).
  • A benchmark (a CI job that prints the actual byte-diff of two successive runs, demonstrating zero-byte drift).
  • A reproducible demo (an axis_demos/ folder with one tiny script per axis, runnable in 5 seconds).
  • A failure case (what happens when this contract breaks — show two reports diverging when BTreeMap is swapped for HashMap).

That last one is rhetorically the strongest. Readers care about non-determinism only when they see it silently break a system. A “what if Locke had a bug?” demo would do more for adoption than three paragraphs about determinism.

13.4 Why this is in the post rather than elsewhere

The deep-dive audit at v0.7+ found four silent-correctness bugs that unit tests didn’t catch — integration tests caught them. The same principle applies here: the substrate is healthy under unit-test scrutiny, but the adoption layer hasn’t been integration-tested against real users yet. The three areas above are the integration findings. Naming them inline keeps the story honest.

The next post in this series will be the end-to-end notebook (D2.1 above) with the belief score visibly degrading across stages, the lineage rendering inline, and the determinism contract demonstrated on a real dataset. That’ll be the “this is what infrastructure actually looks like in use” piece, and it’s the one that needs to exist before Locke can claim “ready for adoption.”

14. Acknowledgements

Pandera is a great library that I use elsewhere and that I’m not knocking. Great Expectations is mature and broadly adopted; the existing benchmark post in this series (Locke v0.1.10) covers GE in more depth. The UCI Adult dataset is Ron Kohavi’s 1996 contribution to the UCI Machine Learning Repository. The synthetic clone in this post is a deterministic stand-in that reproduces the quality issues of Adult; it is not the real dataset.

The Python wrapper is in the CJC-Lang repo at python/ and the Rust crate is at crates/cjc-locke/. Both are MIT-licensed.

item.title: Diabetes-130 Revisited: ABNG Climbs Into the Strong-Model Band — and an Honest Account of What Locke Did (and Didn’t)
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~30 min read
0.6645 ± 0.0018AUC, n=3 seeds, 101K rows
142Locke findings emitted
629/629determinism canaries unmoved
0MLP layers used
TL;DR

A second pass on UCI Diabetes 130-US Hospitals — the same 101,766-encounter readmission dataset from the first ABNG post — with two changes:

  1. Land the validation-tuned config (K_ROUTING = 2, stronger BLR prior) that the first post’s sweep identified but never committed back to source.
  2. Run the full dataset at multiple seeds instead of a 20K sub-sample at a single seed.

Held-out test AUC moves 0.611 → 0.6312 → 0.6621 → 0.6645 ± 0.0018: tuned config at 20K, then full 101K single-seed, then 3-seed variance. 0.6645 is in the strong-published-model band (0.64–0.68) — the band the first post said required nonlinear MLP heads. It turns out the linear BLR + good config + full data was enough.

Locke (the determinism-first data-quality layer) ran the audit. It did not drive the AUC headline. The handoff predicted E9063 multi-class leakage would fire on discharge_disposition_id (death/hospice codes deterministically predict readmitted=NO); it didn’t, because per-feature ROC-AUC misses per-level deterministic outcomes. Applying the prune anyway via domain knowledge dropped AUC by 0.0118 — a confounded experiment, because the prune also shifted MI-selected routing features. Locke’s actual contributions this session were structural: surfacing a patient-encounter leakage hole the first post missed (E9004), exposing its own ?-sentinel blindness on medical CSVs, and pinning a concrete failure mode for a proposed E9064 detector. AUC was ABNG’s win; the systems / detector-roadmap axis was Locke’s.

Prototype disclaimer

ABNG and Locke are both research-stage prototypes built on CJC-Lang, a research compiler that is itself pre-1.0. All three are under active development. The numbers below are a single reproducible experiment campaign — five commits, every chain head verifiable — not a validated benchmark, not deployed, not peer-reviewed. Read as honest exploratory engineering.

Where this sits
  • ABNG: Treating Belief States as First-Class Citizens — the architecture this post measures.
  • ABNG on Real Clinical Data — The Diabetes-130 Readmission Task — Round One. The first post’s AUC 0.611 is the baseline this post improves on.
  • Locke v0.1.10: An Epistemic Layer for Data Validation — Locke’s framing as evidence-aware, determinism-first audit.
  • Benchmarks Part I / Part II — controlled-data baselines.
  • This post — Round Two of the same dataset, now with both tools in the loop, and an honest dissection of what each one actually contributed.

1. What’s new since Round One

The first diabetes-130 post ended with three explicit “still on the bench” items:

  1. Nonlinear leaf heads (MLP) — to see if the ranking ceiling moved.
  2. The full 101,766-row run at the tuned configuration.
  3. Multi-seed variance.

Round Two lands items 2 and 3, leaves item 1 as a design doc, and adds two new ones that the first post didn’t anticipate:

  1. Locke pre-training audit. Run cjcl locke validate on the full CSV, see what the determinism-first data-quality layer surfaces, and act on it.
  2. Belief-weighted per-leaf priors. Use Locke’s per-leaf BeliefScore to weight per-leaf BLR regularization — the directional hypothesis from docs/locke/ABNG_PER_LEAF_BELIEF.md.

That’s the plan. The result is more interesting than the plan, because items 1, 4, and 5 did not work the way the brief expected — and item 1 turned out to be unnecessary for the headline. The honest accounting of which lever moved which number is half the point of this post.

What carries forward unchanged

Determinism contract, audit chain, evaluation protocol — all unchanged. The 70/15/20-style stratified split, fit-on-train-only categorical transform, validation-set-only tuning, single test-set touch, deterministic SplitMix64 RNG, Kahan summation, BTreeMap iteration — all the discipline the first post’s §2 spelled out remains. 629/629 always-run ABNG tests stayed green across five commits of aggressive configuration changes. Every chain head in this post is bit-reproducible. The first post’s §7 reproducibility property — “two runs, 3× wall-clock difference, every metric digit identical” — held throughout.

2. The dataset, briefly — why it’s still hard

The first post’s §1 walked through the dataset in detail. The short version, because the difficulty profile matters for reading the rest of this post:

UCI Diabetes 130-US Hospitals (1999–2008) — 101,766 hospital encounters, 50 columns, deeply heterogeneous. By ML standards it’s genuinely difficult:

  • 101,766 rows × 50 columns. 13 numeric, 23 medication categoricals (one per drug — metformin, insulin, glipizide, etc.), three ICD-9 diagnosis-code columns with 700+ distinct levels each, demographics, admission / discharge / source codes.
  • Missingness sentinel is ? (literal string), not NaN. weight is 96.9% missing. payer_code and medical_specialty are heavily missing. Standard tooling that treats only NaN as missing won’t see any of this without configuration.
  • Longitudinal structure. 16,773 patients have multiple encounters (30,248 duplicate patient_nbr values). Row-level train/test splitting lets the same patient appear in both splits. The first post used row-level splitting; we’ll come back to this in §5.
  • 3-class target (NO / <30 / >30 days to readmission), binarised to <30 vs rest. 11.16% positive class — predicting NO for everyone scores ~89% accuracy with zero learning. Accuracy is the wrong yardstick; the post leads with AUC, balanced accuracy, and calibrated Brier/NLL/ECE.
  • Hard target. Strong published models band at AUC 0.64–0.68. The ceiling is irreducible noise — readmission depends on post-discharge factors (social support, follow-up care, medication adherence) not in the data. A model claiming AUC 0.9 here would be suspect, not impressive.

It’s exactly the kind of dataset where the easy wins (high raw accuracy) are traps, the obvious leakages (death-discharge codes deterministically predicting “no readmission”) hide at the level not the column, and the structural issues (repeated patients) don’t show up in any single AUC number.

3. Landing the tuned config — the first 0.05 AUC

The first post’s §5 ran a validation sweep over K_ROUTING ∈ {2, 3, 4} × BLR prior precision ∈ {0.05, 0.1, 0.5} and concluded:

K = 2 won, decisively. With ~14,000 training rows, K=2 gives 16 leaves (~875 rows each), while K=4 gives 256 leaves (~55 rows each). Deeper routing buys “specialization” but starves every leaf of the data it needs. (Stronger prior regularization also helped, monotonically.)

But the harness’s source code (in tests/abng/dataset_a_diabetes130.rs) still had K_ROUTING = 3 and BLR_PRIOR_PRECISION = 0.1. The sweep result lived in the blog post; it never came home to the harness.

§4.A landed the validated config:

const K_ROUTING: usize = 2;           // was 3
const BLR_PRIOR_PRECISION: f64 = 0.5; // was 0.1

Two constants. No new mechanisms.

What the change did at 20K

The 20K trial (same stratified sub-sample as the first post, seed 42, 80/20 train/test) moved as expected:

Metric Before (K=3, prior=0.1) After (K=2, prior=0.5) First post (K=2, calibrated)
AUC 0.5760 0.6312 0.6107
Brier 0.1049 0.0979 0.0980
NLL 0.5259 0.3666 0.3435
ECE 0.0573 0.0279 0.0101
Populated/total leaves 45 / 64 12 / 16 —
Min/mean/max rows per leaf 1 / 355.5 / 1,583 428 / 1,333.2 / 3,462 —
Routing cols (MI top-K) [17, 9, 21] [17, 9] —
The 20K result is higher than the first post’s

The raw AUC 0.6312 is +0.020 above the first post’s published 0.6107. Two candidate causes:

  1. Different split protocol. The harness uses 80/20 train/test (n_train ≈ 15,999). The first post used 70/15/20 train/val/test (n_train ≈ 14,000). More training data → slightly higher AUC.
  2. Stratified sub-sample RNG path. The harness’s stratified-by-class sub-sample and the first post’s may differ in iteration order; same seed, different exact rows.

This is itself instructive: the first post’s headline 0.611 is not the harness’s ceiling at that config. It was a defensible number under that protocol, but the same model with a different valid evaluation protocol lands higher.

Leaf utilisation is much better too: 12/16 populated (vs 45/64 = 70% before), and the smallest populated leaf has 428 rows (vs 1 — a single-row leaf where a BLR has essentially no information). The mean rises from 355 to 1,333 — every leaf now has enough data to train a meaningful posterior.

time_in_hospital and num_inpatient are the MI-selected routing features at 20K. We’ll come back to that in §5 — they don’t stay the winners at full scale.

4. Locke’s audit — 142 findings, one critical negative result

Before scaling up the data, the brief said: run Locke. Audit the full 101,766-row CSV. Use the findings to drop leakage rows before training. Expected AUC bump: 0.62–0.63 from the prune alone.

The Locke run is one CLI invocation:

./target/release/cjcl.exe locke validate \
    tests/data/diabetes_130/diabetic_data.csv \
    --target readmitted \
    --primary-key patient_nbr \
    --save-json bench_results/diabetes_phase_0_10_section_4b/diabetes_locke_baseline.json \
    --html        bench_results/diabetes_phase_0_10_section_4b/diabetes_locke_baseline.html

It emits 142 findings:

Severity Count Worth acting on this session?
Error 1 YES (see §4.1 below — but it’s a structural concern, not a row filter)
Warning 8 NO (E9082 near-duplicate categorical labels — [0-25) vs [50-75) etc.; real distinctions)
Notice 75 One worth acting on: E9072 ID-like cardinality (already handled by Ignore role)
Info 58 E9002 type-only diagnostics

The single Error is interesting and the negative result is more interesting. Let’s take them in order.

4.1 E9004 — the leakage hole the first post missed

code=E9004 severity=error column=patient_nbr
  message: key column `patient_nbr` has 30248 duplicate values
           across 16773 groups
  evidence:
    - duplicate_keys=30248
    - duplicate_groups=16773

30,248 duplicate patient_nbr values across 16,773 groups — i.e. 16,773 patients had multiple hospital encounters in the dataset. This is correct medical data (real patients return), but it creates a leakage path: if the train/test split is row-level (encounter-level), the same patient can appear in both splits. The model can then learn features about that patient and “look up” their outcome in the test set.

The first post used row-level splitting. So did this Round Two. A truly leakage-free protocol would split by patient_nbr, which would reduce effective training data (more conservative splits typically do) but would tighten the leakage story.

Locke surfaced this without running a single model. AUC numbers wouldn’t have shown it. Whether the encounter-level result is “real signal” or “partial patient-memorisation” can only be decided by re-running with a patient-level split — recorded as future work in §10.

4.2 E9063 — the predicted leakage finding that didn’t fire

The handoff predicted that E9063 (multi-class target leakage) would fire on discharge_disposition_id. The reasoning: codes 11, 13, 14, 19, 20, 21 in that column correspond to death and hospice outcomes. A dead patient can’t be readmitted by construction; those rows are deterministically readmitted = NO. Leakage.

E9063 did not fire. Looking at the detector’s logic (crates/cjc-locke/src/leakage.rs:229), the reason becomes clear: E9063 uses per-feature ROC-AUC (max one-vs-rest) against the multi-class target. Death codes (11, 13, 14, 19, 20, 21) sit interspersed in the numeric range with non-death codes (12, 15, 16, 17, 18). So the column-wide rank statistic doesn’t separate cleanly — most non-death codes also predict NO (89% base rate), so the AUC stays below the 0.85 warning threshold.

The leakage exists at the level — “code 11 → NO with probability ~1.0” — not at the column. ROC-AUC can’t see level-by-level conditional probability.

This is itself a publishable Locke result. A concrete detector gap, pinned to a concrete real-world example:

Proposed E9064 — per-level conditional-probability leakage detector

For each (column, level, target_class) triple, compute P(target = class | column = level). Flag when:

  • P(class \| level) ≥ 0.99 with at least N supports
  • And P(class) (unconditional) < 0.99 — i.e. the level adds new information

This would catch the discharge-code pattern and analogous per-level deterministic-outcome leakages: specific procedure codes that always predict survival, specific lab values that always predict a diagnosis, specific zip codes that always predict insurance class, etc. The data is structured this way more often than column-wide rank-sum detectors admit.

Recorded as a Locke roadmap item with this dataset as the canonical motivating example.

4.3 We applied the prune anyway — and AUC dropped

Domain knowledge still says drop the death-discharge rows. The detector missed; we know better; filter and retry.

Added filter_out_death_discharges in the harness — drops rows where discharge_disposition_id ∈ {11, 13, 14, 19, 20, 21}. That’s 2,423 of 101,766 rows (2.38%), all readmitted = NO. After the filter, the positive-class proportion rises slightly (11.16% → 11.43%).

Re-ran the 20K trial at the same config (K=2, prior=0.5, seed 42):

Metric §4.A (no prune) §4.B (with prune) Δ
AUC 0.6312 0.6194 −0.0118
Brier 0.0979 0.0993 +0.0014
NLL 0.3666 0.3680 +0.0014
ECE 0.0279 0.0229 −0.0050
Bal. accuracy 0.5084 0.5094 +0.0010
Routing cols (MI top-K) [17, 9] [17, 16] CHANGED
Populated leaves 12 / 16 6 / 16 −6
Mean rows/leaf 1,333 2,667 +1,334

Removing leakage rows hurt the model. That looks paradoxical until you read the bottom of the table: the MI-selected routing columns changed. Before the prune, the top-K=2 routing features were [17, 9] = num_inpatient + time_in_hospital. After the prune, they shifted to [17, 16] = num_inpatient + number_emergency. time_in_hospital was a stronger routing feature than number_emergency at 20K — when the prune removes the correlation between time_in_hospital and the target (death patients had specific hospital-stay durations), the MI selector picks the next best feature, which happens to be less useful.

This is a confounded experiment. The §4.B intervention affects both (i) leakage row removal and (ii) downstream routing-feature selection. To isolate (i), you’d need to force the same routing columns across both configs — override the MI selector. That’s not in §4.B’s scope; this is now a documented finding for the next experiment campaign.

But there’s a more interesting insight in the routing change: [17, 16] is the same routing pair the model picks at full 101K scale (§5 below). Removing the leakage rows at 20K shifts MI in the same direction scaling up shifts it. The §4.B negative result is, in part, MI selection drift that was about to happen anyway.

What this means for the handoff hypothesis

The handoff said “Locke surfaces E9063 → drop death rows → AUC up to 0.62–0.63.” Of the three steps:

  • Locke surfaces E9063 — didn’t happen (§4.2; the detector misses this pattern).
  • Drop death rows — happened anyway via domain knowledge.
  • AUC up to 0.62–0.63 — didn’t happen; AUC went down by 0.012.

Three falsified predictions chained — each one its own finding. The chain doesn’t work in this form on this dataset. Whether it works at all on this model class is an open question — see §10.

5. Full 101K, multi-seed — the headline

The first post left “the full 101,766-row run at the tuned configuration” as future work. §4.E delivers it.

5.1 Single seed at full scale

One call to the existing diabetes130_full_run test (which uses budget = usize::MAX, i.e. the entire dataset). Wall clock 213.84 s (≈ 3.6 min). Seed 42.

Metric §4.E (full 101K) §4.A (20K) First post (calibrated, 20K) Strong-model band
AUC 0.6621 0.6312 0.611 0.64–0.68 ✓
Brier 0.0953 0.0979 0.098 —
NLL 0.3349 0.3666 0.344 —
ECE 0.0044 0.0279 0.010 —
Balanced accuracy 0.5012 0.5084 ~0.58 —
F1 @ threshold = 0.5 0.0061 0.0385 ~0.24 —
n_train 81,412 15,999 ~14,000 —
Chain head 56af1961…b3233b6 — — —

AUC 0.6621 — in the strong-published-model band (0.64–0.68). Without any architectural change. Without calibration. Still on the linear BLR head.

The calibration metrics (Brier 0.0953, NLL 0.3349, ECE 0.0044) are all better than the first post’s calibrated values (0.098 / 0.344 / 0.010) — and that comparison is against post-Platt numbers, while these are raw. The increased data volume at the same model class produces raw outputs that are already well-calibrated.

The first post predicted: “Calibration is a monotonic transform, so it cannot change the ranking, and it didn’t… A nonlinear MLP-leaf-head variant is the next experiment — it may lift AUC, or may need a lot of tuning.”

The implication was that AUC ≥ 0.64 required the MLP. It did not. Data scale was enough.

5.2 Routing columns flipped at full scale

The MI-selected routing features at full 101K are [17, 16] — num_inpatient + number_emergency. Not [17, 9] (which won at 20K).

The flip happens because time_in_hospital’s MI advantage over number_emergency is small at 20K and disappears with more data:

Scale Routing cols (MI top-2) Why
20K, no prune [17, 9] time_in_hospital edges out at small scale
20K, with prune (§4.B) [17, 16] Removing death rows kills time_in_hospital’s MI
101K, full [17, 16] At scale, number_emergency wins on its own

This is the architecture’s MI selector doing real work. It picked a different feature pair at a different scale, automatically, no human intervention. Whether time_in_hospital would re-emerge at, say, 50K is an interesting probe — the threshold at which the two cross over is measurable.

5.3 Three seeds — putting an error bar on the headline

A single-seed result is dangerous. §4.F ran the same full-101K pipeline at seeds 42, 43, 44. Wall clock 610.77 s for three runs (~10 min total).

Metric Mean ± Std (n=3) seed 42 seed 43 seed 44
AUC 0.6645 ± 0.0018 0.6621 0.6664 0.6649
Brier 0.0950 ± 0.0002 0.0953 0.0948 0.0950
NLL 0.3346 ± 0.0004 0.3349 0.3340 0.3347
ECE 0.0038 ± 0.0006 0.0044 0.0030 0.0041
Bal. accuracy 0.5019 ± 0.0008 0.5012 0.5015 0.5030
F1 @ 0.5 0.0087 ± 0.0031 0.0061 0.0070 0.0130

The variance is tiny — 0.0018 = 0.27% of the AUC mean. The seed-42 single-seed result (0.6621) was actually below the 3-seed mean (0.6645). Without the multi-seed pass, the single-seed headline understated the model.

All three seeds picked the same routing pair ([17, 16]). Structural decisions are pinned by scale; the per-seed noise is purely the held-out-test stratified sample.

Each seed’s chain head is reproducible bit-for-bit:

seed 42: chain = 56af19614b4dfff6 df97c53949668765 acdc045e316e4fed 087a8a5d9b3233b6
seed 43: chain = 590c3fb936cec418 91576128b82e7243 dc399d6ca49a845c 87bb7392ddb45f7d
seed 44: chain = 89e02f56dadbb651 39418e0ecd3536c0 096b4851ae099694 f3736912ecde2bb2
On F1’s higher relative variance

F1 has the noisiest std (36% of mean — 0.0087 ± 0.0031), and the absolute number is low (0.0087 vs the first post’s calibrated 0.24). Both come from the fixed decision threshold (0.5) on extreme class imbalance — small probability-space shifts at the threshold flip many predictions. The first post tuned the threshold on validation per seed (which got it to ~0.24); we didn’t redo that this session. A per-seed validation-tuned threshold would tighten F1 back to the blog’s number without changing AUC, Brier, or NLL.

This is a reporting choice, not an architectural one. F1 at threshold-0.5 is honest but unflattering; the per-seed-tuned version is honest and the right operating-point report. Both are valid; we deferred the threshold tuning to keep §4.F’s wall clock manageable.

5.4 The honest comparison vs the first post

Metric First post (calibrated, 20K, seed 42) This post (raw, 101K, 3-seed mean) Δ
AUC 0.611 0.6645 ± 0.0018 +0.054
Brier 0.098 0.0950 ± 0.0002 −0.003
NLL 0.344 0.3346 ± 0.0004 −0.009
ECE 0.010 0.0038 ± 0.0006 −0.006

The new numbers beat the first post on every probabilistic axis without Platt calibration. The +0.054 AUC lift is the main story; it puts ABNG in the strong-published-model band on its linear-only configuration.

The first post said:

A nonlinear MLP-leaf-head variant is the next experiment — it may lift AUC, or may need a lot of tuning.

It turns out the bigger lever was data scale, not architecture. We’ll come back to that in §9.

6. The Locke ?-sentinel saga and §4.D

Section 4 of the brief asked for a different Locke contribution: belief-weighted per-leaf BLR priors. The hypothesis (from docs/locke/ABNG_PER_LEAF_BELIEF.md): score each leaf with Locke’s BeliefScore and weight the per-leaf prior accordingly — strong belief → light regularization, weak belief → tight regularization. Operationalises the idea that data-quality variation across the routing tree should drive per-leaf prior strength.

Two blockers surfaced. Part 1 fixed one of them. Part 2 needs ABNG changes that didn’t happen this session.

6.1 Locke didn’t see ? as missing

Running the existing per-leaf belief experiment (tests/abng/per_leaf_belief_diabetes130.rs) produced a per-leaf BeliefScore CSV where every leaf had missingness_score = 1.0000:

leaf_id,n_rows,overall,schema,missingness,drift,leakage,lineage,sample,duplication,constraint
1,9617,0.902500,0.340000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,0.880000
2,6215,0.905000,0.360000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,0.880000
3,2545,0.902500,0.360000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,0.860000
4,1623,0.902500,0.360000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,1.000000,0.860000

Locke is saying: no missingness detected on any leaf. On a dataset where weight is 96.9% ?.

The cause is in Locke’s default ValidationConfig: it treats only f64::NAN as missing for Float columns and produces info-only diagnostics for Str columns (they get an E9002 no null mask supplied notice). Diabetes-130 stores its missing values as the literal string ? — Locke doesn’t see them without explicit configuration.

6.2 The fix — NullMaskMap plumbing

§4.D Part 1 added a harness-side helper that scans every Str column for ? and builds a NullMaskMap:

fn build_question_mark_null_masks(df: &DataFrame) -> NullMaskMap {
    let mut out = NullMaskMap::new();
    for (name, col) in df.columns.iter() {
        if let Column::Str(values) = col {
            let null_rows: Vec<usize> = values.iter().enumerate()
                .filter_map(|(i, v)| if v == "?" { Some(i) } else { None })
                .collect();
            if !null_rows.is_empty() {
                out.insert(name.clone(), NullMask::from_indices(null_rows));
            }
        }
    }
    out
}

Then thread it through ValidateOptions.null_masks. Re-ran the per-leaf belief:

Naive (no null masks) ?-aware
weight recognised as ?-heavy NO YES (96.9% rate)
Columns flagged with ? 0 7
Min per-leaf missingness_score 1.0000 0.9613
Min per-leaf overall 0.9025 0.8977

The belief vector is now informative. Seven columns are correctly flagged as ?-bearing, including weight at 96.9% — matching the first post’s “weight is ~97% missing” observation.

A Locke roadmap item, concretely motivated

Locke should auto-detect common missing-value sentinels. ?, NA, -, NULL, empty string — these are common conventions in medical, financial, and scientific CSVs. Requiring every harness to build a NullMaskMap manually is friction; the cost of false-positive sentinel detection is low (the user can override) and the cost of false-negative is what this section just walked through (a 97%-missing column treated as perfectly clean).

The §4.D Part 1 fix is a one-helper-function workaround that should be in Locke itself. Recorded as a roadmap item.

6.3 The deeper problem — leaves clustered at uniform belief

With ?-handling fixed, the per-leaf belief vector is informative per axis, but all four leaves cluster at near-identical missingness_score:

leaf_id n_rows missingness_score
1 9,617 0.9621
2 6,215 0.9624
3 2,545 0.9621
4 1,623 0.9613

Spread: 0.001. All four leaves are within one-tenth of a percent. That’s because the routing column (time_in_hospital, in this simplified 1-D codebook) doesn’t correlate with data-quality variation. The 97% ? rate in weight is roughly uniform across hospital-stay durations.

For §4.D Part 2 (the actual belief-weighted prior experiment) to do anything, the leaves need to differ on belief. They don’t, on this routing. The directional hypothesis from ABNG_PER_LEAF_BELIEF.md is operationally a no-op when all leaves get the same belief multiplier.

Routing-vs-belief alignment — a research question

Target-MI routing optimises for predictive separation. It picks the features that most reduce uncertainty about the target.

Data-quality routing would optimise for belief separation. It would pick features that most stratify by data quality — different missingness profiles, different correlation structures, different conditional drift.

These are not the same objective. §4.D Part 2 implicitly assumes they align. On this dataset they don’t. Whether they align in general is an open question — it might be that some datasets have data-quality variation that also predicts the target (e.g., underreported labs in a poorly-documented patient subset), in which case the same routing serves both purposes. Or they might be orthogonal, in which case you need separate routing for each.

This is a research-direction question Locke and ABNG together can answer, but neither can answer alone. It needs ABNG to expose a per-leaf prior API and a way to feed belief into routing decisions. Both are multi-session architectural work.

6.4 Part 2 — deferred for a real reason

§4.D Part 2 (apply the belief-weighted prior) requires changes to ABNG’s set_blr_prior:

// Current API — graph-wide, one-shot, must be called before any add_node.
pub fn set_blr_prior(
    &mut self,
    precision: f64,
    a: f64,
    b: f64,
) -> Result<(), GraphError> { ... }

There’s no set_blr_prior_for_node(node_id, precision, a, b). Adding one would touch:

  1. Public API surface (new method on AdaptiveBeliefGraph).
  2. Lifecycle relaxation (must work after add_node, not before).
  3. A new AuditKind::BlrLeafPriorOverride variant for the audit chain.
  4. New SHA-256 canary locks for that event type.

Multi-session work, at the same scope as §4.C MLP heads. Deferred — see §10.

The §4.D Part 1 work is still a real Locke contribution: it makes the per-leaf belief informative on ?-sentinel datasets. The pre-blocker is resolved. The actual experiment (Part 2) is waiting on ABNG architecture work plus the routing-vs-belief alignment question being settled.

7. §4.C MLP heads — design only

The first post named nonlinear MLP leaf heads as the explicit “ranking ceiling” lever. The implicit claim: the linear BLR is the ceiling, calibration can’t move ranking, so to break the 0.611 AUC ceiling you need an MLP head.

§5 of this post already invalidated that framing — full data scale moved AUC into the strong band on the linear head. But MLP heads would still plausibly push higher (0.66 → ~0.68). So the brief asked to start §4.C this session.

The exploration produced a design doc and one critical finding: the MLP forward infrastructure already exists in cjc-abng.

// crates/cjc-abng/src/leaf_head.rs:30
pub struct LeafHead {
    pub input_dim: u32,
    pub hidden_dims: Vec<u32>,   // ← already supports MLP layers
    pub output_dim: u32,
    pub activation: Activation,  // ← Tanh/Sigmoid/Relu/None/Gelu/Silu/Elu/Selu/SinAct
    pub config_hash: [u8; 32],
}

LeafHead::new(in_dim, hidden_dims, out_dim, activation) accepts a Vec<u32> of hidden layer widths. The harness’s call is set_leaf_head(phi_width, vec![], 1, Activation::None) — empty hidden_dims, so the BLR sits directly on raw φ. Setting vec![32] and Activation::Relu would build a 2-layer MLP.

leaf_forward(node_id, x_idx) (graph.rs:1386) already runs the MLP forward inside a cjc-ad::GradGraph, walking layers and applying g.mlp_layer(input, w, b, activation) per layer. leaf_set_param and leaf_set_params_batch already exist for writing trained weights back, and they’re already canary-locked under the existing audit chain.

What’s missing: a training driver. The harness’s train_step (graph.rs:1678) calls blr.update(phi, y) directly — it doesn’t run the MLP forward first. To use an MLP head, you’d need to either:

  1. Write a parallel train_step_with_mlp_forward method on AdaptiveBeliefGraph, or
  2. Do the MLP forward/backward orchestration in the harness, then call the existing train_step with the penultimate features as φ.

Option 2 is ~250 LOC of test-side surgery with no ABNG changes. The design doc spells it out. Probably no new AuditKind variant is required — LeafSetParam/LeafSetParamBatch are already canary-locked.

A scope reframe

The first post said §4.C was “the ranking-ceiling lever — needed to break the 0.61 ceiling.” That framing is now wrong:

  • We’re at AUC 0.6645 ± 0.0018 without MLP. The “ceiling” wasn’t where the framing implied.
  • §4.C’s plausible delta is now ~+0.02 (0.66 → ~0.68), not ~+0.05 (0.61 → 0.66).
  • §4.C is harness-side surgery, not ABNG architecture surgery — the infrastructure already exists.

Recommend Phase 0.11 or a future focused session. The cost/benefit ratio is worse than the first post implied, in both directions: smaller potential lift, but also smaller architectural footprint.

8. ABNG architectural fingerprint — what carried this

The work this post describes uses a purely linear ABNG: BLR heads on raw φ, no nonlinear layers. The result (AUC 0.6645) is, on its face, a result a linear model produced. So a reasonable reader asks: was ABNG architecturally unique on this run, or was it just a linear ensemble in fancy clothes?

The honest answer is that even without MLP, ABNG has an architectural fingerprint that none of scikit-learn’s LogisticRegression, an XGBoost model, or a single per-leaf logistic regression in a tree carries. Five things that mattered on this dataset:

8.1 The φ-vs-routing feature split

ABNG’s categorical transform produces two outputs per row: x (low-dim integer buckets for routing) and phi (high-dim one-hot for the BLR head). The schema’s CategoricalPhiOnly role marks columns as “contribute to φ but never to routing”.

On diabetes-130, that’s what makes the 23 medication columns and 3 ICD-9 codes (700+ levels each) usable. They participate in prediction (φ width stays ≤ 266 across all configs) without exploding the routing tree (which would otherwise need 4^700 leaves). Trees and forests don’t cleanly separate “what splits” from “what predicts” — every feature is a split candidate or excluded. ABNG’s split is at the schema layer, before routing.

8.2 Pre-fixed MI-selected routing

Greedy tree learners pick splits locally — each split optimises against the current data subset. ABNG picks routes globally, once, before any training: score every column’s mutual information against the target, take the top K, build a fixed codebook. The structure is then frozen; training only fills in the per-leaf heads.

This session showed this lever doing real work: at 20K it picked [num_inpatient, time_in_hospital], at 101K it picked [num_inpatient, number_emergency] — automatically, no human tuning. And at 101K all three §4.F seeds picked the same pair: structural decisions are pinned by scale, with seed-to-seed noise concentrated entirely in the per-leaf parameters.

8.3 Per-leaf Bayesian linear regression with conjugate updates

Each leaf is a full Bayesian regression: precision matrix, posterior mean, predictive variance — all updated in closed form per training row. No SGD. No learning rate. No early stopping. O(d²) per step, where d ≈ 266 here.

The root + leaf ensemble (predicted prob = 0.5 × leaf_posterior_mean + 0.5 × root_posterior_mean) is the model that produces 0.6645. Bayesian forests and BART share parts of this idea; the combination of conjugate per-leaf BLRs with frozen pre-MI routing is, as far as I know, ABNG-specific.

8.4 SHA-256 audit chain over training events

Every train_step appends an event to a hash chain. End-state chain head is byte-reproducible. Merkle root verifies. This is the property no commodity ML system carries.

In this session: five commits, each carrying a chain head. Aggressive config changes throughout — constants flipped, new tests added, a new filter applied, multi-seed runs. The 28 SHA-256 determinism canaries didn’t move once, because nothing they cover was touched. The work was safe to be exploratory in a way most ML harnesses don’t permit.

8.5 Bit-identical replay

The first post’s reproducibility evidence (“Run 1 / Run 2 — every metric digit identical, 3× wall-clock difference”) held throughout Round Two. The §4.F std of 0.0018 is direct evidence: variance comes entirely from seed-driven sub-sample / split / training-order permutations, not from numerical noise. Kahan summation, BTreeMap iteration (not HashMap), no FMA in SIMD kernels, deterministic SplitMix64 RNG — the determinism contract is a system-wide invariant.

The five chain heads from this session — 8897c2dd… (before), 57463728… (§4.A after), 78a63f56… (§4.B pruned), 56af1961… (§4.E full), and the three §4.F seeds — are durable artifacts. Anyone with the codebase, the seed, and the dataset can rerun and confirm bit-identity.

What the MLP would have added

One thing: nonlinearity inside each leaf. The linear leaf head can only fit linear relationships between φ and the target within its routing slice. An MLP head would let each leaf learn interactions (medication × demographic × diagnosis).

But the routing tree itself is already a form of nonlinearity — splitting the data into 16 leaves by num_inpatient × number_emergency means each leaf sees a piecewise-linear slice of the data. The MLP would push from piecewise-linear to per-leaf-arbitrary-nonlinear. The §4.C design doc estimates that buys ~0.02 AUC (0.66 → ~0.68) — a real but bounded lift.

The architectural fingerprint above (φ-routing split, frozen MI routing, conjugate BLR heads, audit chain, bit-replay) doesn’t depend on the MLP — it’s the substrate the MLP would sit on. The fingerprint is what made the exploration safe, the determinism contract durable, and the per-leaf result interpretable. The headline number was driven by data scale + tuning, not the substrate; the substrate is what made the work auditable.

9. Honest assessment — what Locke did, and didn’t

If §8 is “ABNG worked end-to-end,” this section is the matching honest accounting for Locke. The brief’s flagship hypothesis was “Locke surfaces leakage → drop rows → AUC up.” That didn’t happen. So what did Locke contribute?

Six roles, six honest takes (from the session notes):

Numerical Computing axis

Locke did not directly drive the AUC bump. Every gain — §4.A’s +0.055, §4.E’s +0.031, §4.F’s confidence bound — came from ABNG tuning + data scale, not any Locke finding. The flagship §4.B hypothesis (Locke surfaces E9063 → drop death rows → AUC up) didn’t pan out: E9063 didn’t fire, and the domain-knowledge prune we applied anyway hurt AUC by 0.0118.

Lead Architect axis

Locke surfaced its own detector ceiling, which is a publishable finding in itself. E9063 is per-feature ROC-AUC; it misses per-level deterministic leakage. The diabetes-130 dataset is the kind of medical data where leakage hides at the level (discharge code 11 = expired), not the column. The proposed E9064 per-level conditional-probability detector is now a concrete Locke roadmap item, bound to a real-world example.

Determinism Auditor axis

Locke’s determinism contract held — cjcl locke verify --runs N would still pass on every artifact produced. The 142 findings and their content-addressed IDs are reproducible. The audit chain integrity is preserved across an exploratory session that changed configuration substantially. This is a non-trivial systems contribution: a noisy, exploratory ML harness changed configs aggressively, and the data-side audit trail never needed to budge.

Pipeline Engineer axis

Locke’s CLI surface worked exactly as designed. --target readmitted --primary-key patient_nbr --save-json --html ran to completion, produced structured JSON, and emitted a navigable HTML report with the Pearson correlation heatmap. The plumbing is solid. No CLI or schema issues.

Runtime Systems axis

Locke’s findings flagged structural concerns AUC numbers couldn’t see. Two examples:

  1. E9004 on patient_nbr — 30,248 duplicate keys across 16,773 groups. Means the harness’s encounter-level train/test split lets the same patient appear in train + test. AUC won’t show it; Locke surfaced it without running a single model.
  2. E9082 on weight / medical_specialty / max_glu_serum — near-duplicate category strings. Some are real distinctions ([0-25) vs [50-75)), but the warning forces a deliberate “yes these are different, not typos” review.
