  • Calendar-aware time parsing. Today “last 3 months” approximates as 90 days; a dedicated parser makes it exact and is reused by every time-based question.
  • An approved-functions registry — the governance-safe path to UDF-like power: author-vetted functions the NL layer can select, never emit.
  • Embedding retrieval, via the seam above.

Closing

The charts are the demo; the row-filter fix is the substance. Adding a visible feature is what dragged a latent correctness bug in the boring infrastructure into the light — a pattern that shows up again and again in governed-data systems. Gibran’s bet is still the same one v0.1.0 made: trade natural-language breadth for hard correctness guarantees, and weave governance into the SQL so there’s no boundary to escape across. This release makes that bet hold for one more class of query.

  • GitHub: https://github.com/AdamEzzat1/gibran
  • Previous post: Gibran v0.1.0: Natural-Language Analytics Without an LLM

Verification: 807 Python tests passing, plus a TypeScript type-check, production build, and 26 frontend component tests. The SQL shown is illustrative — real output uses fully-qualified, quoted identifiers.

item.title: Qalam: A Deterministic Arabic Morphology Engine — and a Way to Learn Arabic
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~9 min read
5Rust crates
98tests passing
0bits differ across 3 OSes
1language I’m learning
TL;DR

Qalam is a deterministic Arabic morphological analyzer I’m building in Rust, structured like a compiler pipeline (text → normalize → tokenize → clitics → morphology). Today it reliably normalizes, tokenizes, and builds variant-aware word-frequency tables over any Arabic text, and it does real morphology — splitting clitics and extracting roots (strong and weak), each with an explainable confidence and an audit trail. The catch: morphology only confirms the ~99 roots in its bootstrap lexicon, which on arbitrary text is about a quarter of words. It’s bit-identical across Linux, macOS, and Windows, enforced in CI. It is not a finished tool — no big lexicon yet, no syntax, no dialect, no measured accuracy. And the honest truth: half the reason it exists is that building it forces me to actually learn Arabic.

Why I’m building this

I’ll start with the part that isn’t an engineering decision: I’m learning Arabic, and I learn far better when I have a concrete goal. Flashcards and grammar drills bounce off me. But “make the analyzer recover the root of قال correctly” is a problem I can’t hand-wave my way through — I have to actually understand that قال comes from the root ق-و-ل, that the middle و surfaces as a long ا, and that without context you genuinely cannot tell whether that ا is hiding a و or a ي.

So Qalam is a passion project with a double payoff. Every feature I add is a grammar lesson I’m forced to internalize, because the code won’t work until I do. Arabic morphology is unusually well-suited to this: it’s systematic in a way that rewards a programmer’s instincts — words are built from three-consonant roots poured into patterns (أوزان), like كَاتِب (writer) and مَكْتُوب (written) and كِتَاب (book) all sharing the root ك-ت-ب. That structure is almost begging to be modeled as code. Building the model is how I’m learning the language.

What Qalam is

Qalam treats Arabic the way a compiler treats source code: as a series of typed intermediate representations, each lowering into the next.

raw text
  → Unicode normalization      (NFC + Arabic letter folds)
  → tokenization               (script-aware, raw-anchored spans)
  → clitic splitting           (peel و/ف/ب/ك/ل/س/ال and pronoun suffixes)
  → morphology                 (templatic patterns → root extraction)
  → lexicon confirmation       (promote real roots, suppress spurious ones)
  → a ranked analysis "forest"

The non-negotiable design principle is determinism: the same input plus the same lexicon plus the same config produces byte-identical output, on every machine, every time — with a provenance trail explaining why each interpretation was produced. Ambiguity (and Arabic is full of it) is never silently resolved; it’s enumerated as a ranked forest of candidates, each carrying a confidence and the evidence behind it.

The code lives in five small Rust crates: qalam-core (the IR types), qalam-text (normalization, tokenization, clitics), qalam-morph (patterns, roots, the analyzer, the lexicon), qalam-lexicon-builder, and qalam-cli.

Why Rust

A few reasons, and they’re specific to this project rather than generic Rust enthusiasm:

  • Determinism is controllable. The whole pitch is bit-reproducibility, and Rust lets me choose data structures with that in mind: BTreeMap where iteration order must be stable, no garbage collector introducing timing-dependent behavior, no hidden hashing randomization unless I opt into it. The output forest is sorted by explicit, total keys. In a language with more “convenient” defaults, I’d be fighting nondeterminism the whole way.
  • The IR design maps onto Rust’s strengths. A layered analyzer is mostly enums and exhaustive pattern matching — TokenKind, Pos, Slot, WeakResolution are all enums, and the compiler forces me to handle every case. That’s exactly the discipline a morphology engine needs.
  • Reproducible artifacts across platforms. Rust’s defined numeric behavior plus a single compiled binary means I can prove the cross-OS guarantee — and I do, in CI, by diffing the analyzer’s output across Linux, macOS, and Windows on every push.
  • No GPU, no runtime, fast. Morphology is rule-and-table work, not matrix math. Rust gives me speed and a tiny footprint without a heavyweight runtime.
  • It sharpens both skills at once. I’m strengthening my Rust and my Arabic on the same project. That’s efficient.

What it can do today (and I’ve checked every claim below)

There are really two tiers of capability, and being honest about the line between them matters.

Tier 1 — full coverage, works on any text. Normalization, tokenization, and frequency analysis don’t need a lexicon, so they work on arbitrary Arabic:

  • qalam normalize applies Unicode NFC plus an Arabic fold table — collapsing the alef variants (أ إ آ ٱ → ا) and stripping tatweel (the ـــ elongation) — while preserving diacritics, teh marbuta, and the hamza-bearing letters. It’s idempotent.
  • qalam freq builds a word-frequency table grouped by normalized form, so أحمد and احمد are correctly counted as the same word and you can see which raw spellings collapsed together.
  • qalam segment shows the clitic-splitting forest for each word.

Tier 2 — real morphology, but only as far as the lexicon reaches. qalam analyze runs the whole pipeline. Here’s its actual output on a handful of words (verbatim — root= is the extracted root, pat= the matched pattern, the leading number is confidence):

الكتاب   0.782  ال · كتاب   root=ك-ت-ب  pat=4    (the book — article peeled, root found)
مكتوب    0.808  مكتوب       root=ك-ت-ب  pat=3    (written — passive participle مفعول)
مدرسة    0.790  مدرسة       root=د-ر-س  pat=8    (school — place noun مفعلة)
قال      0.790  قال         root=ق-و-ل  pat=20   (he said — hollow weak root)
قِ        0.880  قِ          root=و-ق-ي  pat=—    (protect! — see below)
في       0.940  في          root=—      (particle — function word, no root)

Those roots are all correct. And it works without diacritics, because the written long vowels and pattern letters pin the alignment — كاتب and كتاب both resolve to ك-ت-ب even though neither carries a single short vowel mark.

And it’s deterministic. A CI job runs qalam normalize, freq, and analyze over a fixed corpus on Linux, macOS and macOS-on-ARM, and Windows, then diffs the outputs byte-for-byte. They have never differed — including the floating-point confidence values. That cross-architecture bit-equality is the property I care about most, and it’s machine-checked on every commit.

The honest limitation: coverage

Here’s the number that frames everything. I ran analyze on a neutral, non-cherry-picked MSA sentence — a generic news-style line about ministries and infrastructure. Of 17 tokens, exactly 4 got a lexicon-confirmed root. That’s roughly a quarter of the content words.

Why? The bootstrap lexicon currently holds 99 roots, 37 particles, and 5 irregular forms. Arabic has thousands of roots and hundreds of thousands of words. So on real text, most words fall back to either an unconfirmed pattern guess (which I deliberately down-weight, because it’s often spurious) or nothing at all. The engine is correct on what it knows; it just doesn’t yet know much.

Two more honest caveats:

  • There is no accuracy benchmark. I can’t tell you “Qalam is X% accurate,” because I haven’t evaluated it against a gold-standard treebank. The confidence numbers above are deterministic, hand-tuned priors — useful for ranking, not calibrated probabilities.
  • It does not beat the established tools. MADAMIRA, Farasa, and CAMeL Tools have enormous lexicons and statistical models; on coverage and accuracy they win, easily. Qalam’s only real edges are determinism, explainability, and being a clean, dependency-light Rust codebase — which matter to a niche (reproducible research, auditable pipelines, and people learning the language), not to the broad market.

The part I’m proudest of: weak roots

If you’ve studied Arabic, you know the “weak” roots — the ones containing و, ي, or ء — are where the tidy three-consonant system gets messy. The radical mutates, lengthens, or vanishes entirely. This is the part that’s genuinely hard to do deterministically, and it’s also the part that taught me the most.

Qalam handles the main classes by enumerate-then-disambiguate:

  • Hollow roots (weak middle radical) surface with a long ا: قال could be ق-و-ل or ق-ي-ل — the surface honestly doesn’t say which. So the engine produces both candidates, and the lexicon breaks the tie: ق-و-ل is a known root, ق-ي-ل isn’t, so the real one is promoted (0.79) and the phantom is demoted (0.12).
  • Defective roots (weak final radical) are luckier — ا vs ى actually distinguishes them, so دعا → د-ع-و and رمى → ر-م-ي are directly recoverable.
  • Assimilated roots (weak initial radical) like وصل → و-ص-ل are visible and straightforward.

And then there’s the one I included precisely because it’s the hardest: قِ, the imperative of و-ق-ي (“protect”), famous as possibly the shortest complete sentence in Arabic — a single letter. There is no rule that recovers و-ق-ي from a lone ق; it’s pure lexical knowledge. So I handled it the only honest way: a small table of irregular forms, documented as exactly that — a lexical exception, not a morphophonemic rule. analyze قِ returns و-ق-ي. Pretending a rule could derive it would have been a lie, and the whole project is built on not telling those.

What it can’t do (yet)

To be completely clear about the boundaries:

  • No large lexicon. This is the single biggest gap, and it’s data work, not code — sourcing and license-cleaning open Arabic corpora. It’s what stands between “a quarter of words” and “most words.”
  • No diacritization. It reads partially- or un-diacritized text fine, but it doesn’t generate the vowel marks.
  • No Egyptian dialect yet. Egyptian↔︎MSA normalization is designed but unbuilt — and it’s the second half of the project’s whole reason for being.
  • No syntax, no semantics, no knowledge graph, no search, no learning engine. These are all designed on paper as later phases. None exist yet.

The morphology engine is roughly feature-complete for a v0.1 — strong roots, weak roots, clitics, particles, irregulars. What it lacks is data and the higher layers.

What I measured vs. what I’m claiming

In the spirit of not overselling:

  • Measured / verified: the analyze outputs above (reproduced verbatim), the 98 passing tests, the 5-crate layout, the lexicon counts (99/37/5), the 4-of-17 coverage probe, and the cross-OS byte-identical determinism (machine-checked in CI).
  • A deterministic prior, not a calibrated probability: every confidence number. They rank candidates sensibly; they are not validated against ground truth.
  • Not measured at all: accuracy. No treebank evaluation exists yet, so I make no accuracy claim.
  • Designed, not built: dialect normalization, syntax, semantic IR, knowledge graph, search, the learning engine, and the full lexicon ingestion.

The road ahead

The next move is almost forced: the lexicon. Nothing else lifts that “quarter of words” number — not dialect, not syntax, not search. They all sit on top of morphology, which sits on top of the lexicon. So the plan is to build the ingestion pipeline (open sources → a content-hashed, memory-mapped FST the analyzer already knows how to consume) so that the data, when it arrives, is a drop-in that makes every other capability better at once.

But I want to end where I started, because it’s the honest center of the project. Qalam is not trying to beat MADAMIRA. It’s a deterministic, explainable, carefully-engineered Arabic morphology engine wrapped around a deliberately small lexicon — and it’s the thing that’s actually getting me to learn Arabic. Every weak-root rule I implemented, I now understand, in a way no flashcard ever managed. Building the tool turned out to be the lesson.

item.title: Green by Construction: Thermal Bounds Without Losing a Bit
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~19 min read
3thermal profiles
80new tests
1.8×burst recovered
0output bits changed
TL;DR

I gave CJC-Lang a green-compute layer in three phases. Phase 1 is a Runtime Policy Layer: --profile cool|balanced|max-perf bounds how much CPU a run uses, plus a deterministic energy estimate. Phase 2 is race-to-idle adaptive scheduling — full speed for short bursts, throttle only when load is sustained — which recovered ~1.8× of the throughput the fixed cap cost, while keeping the sustained thermal bound. Phase 3a is fused elementwise kernels that eliminate intermediate tensor allocations. The whole thing rests on one invariant: thread count, pool size, and the adaptive schedule provably never change the output. Same seed, same bits — across every profile, thread count, and both executors. That’s what makes an adaptive thermal knob safe in a language whose entire pitch is reproducibility.

The problem: a laptop that cooks itself

CJC-Lang’s core promise is same seed, same output, always. To get there it already does the unglamorous work: ordered collections, Kahan/binned summation, a seeded RNG, and — critically for this story — no FMA in the SIMD kernels, so a multiply-then-add rounds the same way every time.

But nothing in the runtime bounded how hard it pushed the machine. The parallel matmul and reduction kernels ran on Rayon’s default global pool — every core, all the time. On a laptop, a training loop would peg all 8 cores, spin the fans up, and eventually thermal-throttle: the CPU gets hot enough that the firmware lowers the clock to protect itself, and you end up slower than if you’d never saturated it.

I wanted an explicit, deterministic answer to “how much machine may this run use?” — without the OS power governor’s opacity, and without breaking the reproducibility contract.

The reframe that makes it tractable

Here’s the mental model I kept coming back to:

Energy = power × time. Capping cores cuts power but raises time. On a short burst that’s often a wash — you run cooler but longer. The levers that recover performance and save energy attack the other terms: total work (fewer cycles), memory traffic (the dominant energy term), and when you spend the thermal budget.

Crucially: bursts don’t overheat — only sustained load does. Thermal time constants are seconds to tens of seconds, so a sub-second burst at full tilt never heat-soaks the package. A fixed thread cap pays the throttle tax on every operation, including the short interactive runs that were never going to overheat.

And the linchpin that makes all of this safe: in CJC-Lang, thread count never changes the result. The parallel reductions sum in a fixed chunk order with Kahan/binned accumulators, so the numeric output is identical no matter how many workers are live. That single guarantee is what lets me treat parallelism as a pure heat/speed knob — and even make it adaptive — without ever risking the answer.

What determinism does not buy you

I want to be careful here, because there’s an easy and wrong way to tell this story: “determinism makes computation greener.” It doesn’t — not directly, and per-run it’s often the opposite. Kahan and binned summation do more arithmetic than a naive sum. A seeded RNG, ordered collections, audit trails, and bit-exact replay all cost extra cycles and memory. Measured one run at a time, “trustworthy” is more expensive than “fast and loose,” not less.

So the green argument is not “determinism reduces FLOPs.” It’s narrower and, I think, more defensible: determinism, numerical stability, and bounded execution reduce the number of runs you waste on the way to a result you can actually trust. The savings live in the denominator — fewer reruns — not the numerator. Everything below should be read in that light.

Phase 1: a Runtime Policy Layer

The first piece is a RuntimePolicy — the green-compute control surface. It lives in a thread-local in cjc-runtime, mirroring the existing profiler sink.

pub struct RuntimePolicy {
    pub determinism: Determinism,   // Strict
    pub numeric_mode: NumericMode,  // Kahan | Binned | FixedTree
    pub thermal_mode: ThermalMode,  // Cool | Balanced | MaxPerf
    pub max_threads: usize,         // 0 = auto (derive from thermal_mode)
    pub batch_size: usize,
    pub audit_mode: AuditMode,      // Summary | Full | Forensic
    pub adaptive: bool,             // race-to-idle (Phase 2)
}

Three thermal profiles, exposed as CLI flags applied once at startup so they cover run, bench, and every command:

Profile Threads (8-core host) Batch Use
cool ≈ ¼ cores → 2 32 laptop, edge, gentle
balanced (default) ≈ ½ cores → 4 128 normal — leaves headroom
max-perf all → 8 512 benchmarking

The default is balanced, deliberately not “all cores forever.”

A deterministic energy estimate

Most energy tools (CodeCarbon, Scaphandre, RAPL counters) measure power from hardware — which is non-deterministic and machine-specific. I wanted energy to be a number you can assert in a test. So energy_estimate is a pure function of the workload, never of wall-clock time:

joules ≈ flops × 1e-10  +  bytes × 1e-10
         (~100 pJ/flop)     (~100 pJ/byte, DRAM-ish)

The two terms are computed in separate bindings so the compiler can’t fuse them into an FMA — the same no-FMA discipline as the SIMD kernels. Same program, same seed → same FLOP count → same joules, bit-for-bit. On the branch, energy_estimate(1000000, 2000) returns 0.00010020000000000001 — identical under every profile, thread count, and both executors. The absolute number is an order-of-magnitude estimate; the ratio between two runs is the meaningful, reproducible figure.

What the fixed cap costs

Measured on an 8-core machine, release build, cjcl bench (10 runs) on a 1024×1024 matmul:

Profile Threads Mean
--threads 1 1 628 ms
cool 2 468 ms
balanced (default) 4 268 ms
max-perf 8 210 ms

So the cap genuinely throttles (max-perf is ~3× faster than single-threaded) — and the laptop-safe default costs ~27% versus all-out. That cost is real, and Phase 2 is about clawing most of it back.

Phase 2: race-to-idle adaptive scheduling

A fixed cap throttles short and long workloads alike. Race-to-idle fixes that: run at full width during a burst, and throttle to the cap only once load is sustained (a 2-second window; a ½-second idle gap resets it).

The mechanism is the interesting part. Rayon’s global pool can only be configured once, so I don’t shrink it — I leave it at full size so bursts can use every core. Throttling happens per-operation: when a workload has been busy past the window, the kernel’s parallel section is install-ed into a cached, cap-sized pool. Inside that pool rayon::current_num_threads() reports the cap, so the existing band/row/chunk splitters auto-scale — no per-kernel rewrites.

pub fn run_parallel<R: Send>(work: impl FnOnce() -> R + Send) -> R {
    if rayon::current_thread_index().is_some() { return work(); } // nested → inline
    let cap = effective_threads(&get(), detect_cores());
    if cap >= detect_cores() { return work(); }                   // no cap
    let throttle = if get().adaptive { is_sustained_now() } else { true };
    if !throttle { return work(); }                               // burst → full pool
    match capped_pool(cap) { Some(p) => p.install(work), None => work() }
}

The four parallel kernels (large/medium/batch matmul, SIMD elementwise) wrap their bodies in run_parallel.

Did it work? Two regimes, measured

Short workload (one 1024² matmul, finishes inside the burst window), minimum of 9 --time runs:

cool fixed cool adaptive max-perf
time 0.353 s 0.196 s 0.153 s

Adaptive-cool recovered ~1.8× (0.353 → 0.196 s) — landing within fixed startup overhead of max-perf. The short, interactive case now runs at full speed and never trips the cap.

Sustained workload (25× 1024² matmuls, ~runs well past the 2 s window):

cool fixed cool adaptive max-perf
time 16.7 s 10.8 s 4.2 s

This is the part I care about most: adaptive-cool (10.8 s) sits between fixed-cool and max-perf. It is not near max-perf (4.2 s) — meaning it genuinely throttled after the burst. The thermal bound held; the burst just bought back the first couple of seconds. Exactly the goal: fast when it’s safe, bounded when it matters.

Why this is safe

The burst-vs-throttle decision reads the wall clock, so the schedule is non-deterministic run-to-run. But the output isn’t, because the choice only changes how many bands run concurrently — never the per-element math. To prove it rather than assert it: a 256×256 matrix product (a real parallel reduction over varied values) hashed byte-identical across --threads 1, cool, balanced, max-perf, adaptive on/off, and both the AST and MIR executors:

threads=1  (eval)    : 79aaf9931d2b6c2004a20cdf5d5bd434
threads=8  (eval)    : 79aaf9931d2b6c2004a20cdf5d5bd434
cool (adaptive burst): 79aaf9931d2b6c2004a20cdf5d5bd434
cool --no-adaptive   : 79aaf9931d2b6c2004a20cdf5d5bd434
max-perf             : 79aaf9931d2b6c2004a20cdf5d5bd434
MIR cool (adaptive)  : 79aaf9931d2b6c2004a20cdf5d5bd434

For reproducible benchmarking — where you want a fixed schedule — --no-adaptive applies the cap uniformly.

Phase 3a: fused kernels cut memory traffic

The third lever is memory. A plain a*b + c allocates an intermediate: mul → a fresh tensor, then add → another. That’s two allocations and ~5N words of traffic for an N-element result. A fused single-pass kernel does it in one allocation and one pass (~4N words).

I added three, joining the existing broadcast_fma:

Builtin Computes Replaces
fused_axpy(α, x, y) α·x + y scalar-mul + add (BLAS axpy)
fused_mul_sub(a, b, c) a·b − c mul + sub
fused_sub_sq(a, b) (a−b)² sub + mul (MSE/variance/distance)

The subtle bit is why this is free: a*b + c in Rust compiles to a separate multiply then add — it does not contract into a hardware FMA unless you call .mul_add() explicitly. So the fused loop produces bit-identical results to the two-pass version while eliminating the intermediate. I pinned that with property tests asserting fused == unfused byte-for-byte over 256 random cases each.

And this compounds with Phase 2: lighter work finishes inside the burst window, so the cap engages even less. Memory efficiency and the adaptive scheduler reinforce each other instead of trading off.

Where the waste actually is

If determinism makes each run a little more expensive, why is any of this “green”? Because the dominant source of wasted compute in real numerical and ML work isn’t the cost of one run — it’s runs you have to throw away. Unstable, irreproducible systems waste compute, and they waste a lot of it:

  • Nondeterministic drift. A number changes between runs and you can’t tell whether it’s a bug or noise — so you rerun, sometimes dozens of times, just to characterize the variance.
  • NaN / instability cascades. An unstable reduction overflows or loses precision, a training run diverges, and everything downstream is garbage. You debug, patch, and rerun the whole pipeline.
  • Failed reproductions. A reviewer, a teammate, or future-you can’t reproduce a result, so the experiment is repeated from scratch.
  • Preprocessing redone because the previous run wasn’t pinned.

Every one of those is compute already spent that produced nothing trustworthy. That’s the waste CJC-Lang is actually attacking — with stable reductions, a seeded RNG, bit-exact replay, and now bounded, memory-aware execution. None of it makes a single run cheaper. It makes reruns unnecessary.

An honest metric: trustworthy results per joule

Throughput (FLOPs/second) is the wrong yardstick for this. A better one is closer to:

trustworthy_results / joule — useful, reproducible outputs per unit of energy, counting the energy burned on runs you had to repeat.

I ran a small experiment to make it concrete. A 512×512 matmul, with the deterministic energy estimate from Phase 1:

result summary  : 2307326611210371000   p.sum()
energy estimate : 0.0274726912 J         energy_estimate(2·512³ flops, 3·512²·8 bytes)

Then I checked reproducibility — the same program across every profile, thread cap, adaptive on/off, and both executors:

e81183611e12dd95d56e79b9b36ff194   default
e81183611e12dd95d56e79b9b36ff194   --profile max-perf
e81183611e12dd95d56e79b9b36ff194   --profile cool
e81183611e12dd95d56e79b9b36ff194   --profile cool --no-adaptive
e81183611e12dd95d56e79b9b36ff194   --threads 1
e81183611e12dd95d56e79b9b36ff194   --mir-opt

Identical, every time. So:

  • One run yields a result you can trust → ~0.0275 J per trustworthy result (≈ 36 results/joule, at this analytical estimate).
  • Certifying it — producing it and independently re-running to confirm bit-equality — costs two runs (~0.055 J) and reaches actual certainty.

Now the honest part, because this is exactly where the story could overreach. That 0.0275 J is the numerator, and determinism did not shrink it — the stable reductions arguably made it slightly bigger. The metric’s real lever is the rerun multiplier: if establishing a trustworthy result takes k runs, you spend k × 0.0275 J. In a nondeterministic pipeline chasing drift or instability, k can be many; here it is 1, and verification is exact replay rather than statistics-over-noise. I’m deliberately not fabricating a competitor’s k — I can’t measure someone else’s pipeline, and I’m not going to pretend to. The claim is narrow and checkable: CJC-Lang drives k toward 1, and that — not FLOPs-per-run — is where the energy is saved.

(Two caveats stack here: the joules are an analytical estimate, not a wattmeter reading, and the rerun multiplier is a model, not a benchmark of another system. Both are stated so you can discount them appropriately.)

Why bounded heat matters beyond one run

The thermal bound isn’t only about the run in front of you. Three stakes, in decreasing order of how confidently I can claim them:

  • Short run — the throttle cliff (measured). Saturate every core long enough and the firmware lowers the clock to protect the chip, so a thermally-unbounded run can finish slower than a bounded one. Capping sustained load keeps you on the right side of that cliff.
  • Long run — hardware stress (physics, not a benchmark). Sustained operation near thermal limits accelerates component aging — degradation roughly follows an Arrhenius curve (≈2× per 10 °C), and thermal cycling fatigues solder joints and packaging. Running cooler reduces that stress. I haven’t measured device lifetimes — that’s a multi-year claim — so I’ll state it as physics, not a result.
  • At scale — heat is cooling cost. In a datacenter, a large slice of energy goes to removing the heat compute produces (it’s a big term in PUE). Bounding sustained load lowers the cooling burden — it complements cooling rather than replacing it (you still buy the heatsink). On a laptop the same lever buys quieter fans and longer battery.

And one correction worth making explicit, because it’s easy to get backwards: heat threatens performance, not the answer. Thermal throttling changes the clock, which changes timing — never the bits. CJC-Lang’s output is timing-independent by construction (fixed reduction order, seeded RNG, no result-affecting races), so a throttled run is byte-identical to a cool one, just slower. What heat actually breaks is performance reproducibility — a hot benchmark isn’t repeatable. So the two guarantees are separate and complementary: the thermal bound protects how fast you go; determinism protects what you get — and it survives the chip throttling. (In a non-deterministic, timing-dependent system, throttling-induced timing shifts genuinely can perturb results — which is exactly the failure mode CJC-Lang is built not to have.)

How unique is this?

Honest answer: the individual pieces all exist somewhere. Thread caps are everywhere (Rayon, OpenMP, GOMAXPROCS). OS governors and RAPL do power capping. Race-to-idle is textbook power management. CodeCarbon and friends estimate energy. Intel MKL has reproducible-numerics modes.

What I haven’t seen packaged together is the combination at the language-runtime level with a determinism guarantee: a thermal/energy policy where the knobs are provably result-invariant, paired with an adaptive scheduler and a reproducible energy estimate. In most stacks, changing thread count can change floating-point results; here it provably can’t, which is the whole reason an adaptive thermal scheduler is even safe to ship. It’s most at home in the niche CJC-Lang targets — reproducible scientific, regulated, and edge computing — not hyperscale throughput.

What I measured vs. what I estimated

In keeping with the project’s ethos, the honest accounting:

  • Measured: all the wall-clock numbers (release cjcl bench / --time), the determinism hashes, the test counts. These are real and on the branch.
  • A proxy, not a measurement: I measured wall-clock and active-core counts, not watts or junction temperature. Fewer active cores means less package power, but I don’t have hardware sensors in the loop — treat the thermal claims as a well-founded proxy, not a power reading.
  • Analytical, not benchmarked: the fused-kernel traffic reduction (~5N → ~4N words, two allocations → one) is from counting, not a wall-clock microbenchmark. What I did verify is that the output is bit-identical to the unfused path.
  • Deferred: the data-DSL group-by/join memory work (Phase 3b) is not in this post. The obvious approach turned out to change output order (the streaming path sorts keys; the materializing path preserves first-occurrence order), which would break determinism — so I stopped rather than ship a correctness regression. It needs an order-preserving design, which is its own phase.

Verification

Every claim above is backed by tests added for this work: 80 new tests — 20 unit and 43 integration for the policy/scheduling layer, plus 17 for the fused kernels — spanning unit, dual-executor parity (AST vs MIR), property (256 cases each), and fuzz checks. The existing suite stayed green and no determinism gate moved.

A note on tone, because it’s easy to get this wrong: none of this makes CJC-Lang “environmentally superior” to anything. It’s far too early for that claim, and against a hyperscale GPU cluster it isn’t even the right comparison. The honest framing is narrower and more interesting as a research question — CJC-Lang is exploring whether deterministic, memory-aware, thermally-bounded systems can reduce wasteful computation (the reruns, the drift-debugging, the throwaway experiments) in the niches where trust matters more than peak throughput. That’s the hypothesis this work is testing, not a verdict.

The throughline: you don’t have to choose between fast, reproducible, and cool. You can have a thermal bound that adapts to your workload, energy you can reason about, and lower memory traffic — and still get the same bits out every single time. In a deterministic language, that last clause isn’t a nice-to-have. It’s the whole point.

item.title: ABNG on Real Clinical Data — The Diabetes-130 Readmission Task
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~13 min read
TL;DR

I put ABNG — the Adaptive Belief Network Graph, an experimental, deterministic, auditable belief-graph model built in CJC-Lang — onto a real medical dataset: UCI Diabetes 130-US Hospitals, 101,766 hospital encounters, predicting 30-day readmission. It’s a genuinely hard, heterogeneous, ~11%-imbalanced clinical benchmark.

Held-out test AUC went 0.550 → 0.587 → 0.611 as I scaled the data and ran a validation-only hyperparameter sweep. Post-hoc Platt calibration then moved the probabilistic metrics from worse than the do-nothing baseline to better than it — Brier 0.100 → 0.098, log-loss 0.391 → 0.344, calibration error 0.031 → 0.010.

The honest headline: none of ABNG’s core architecture changed. The gains came from a leakage-free evaluation protocol, tuning ABNG’s configuration, and a harness-side calibration step. Determinism and the audit chain held throughout — precisely because the core was left untouched. AUC 0.611 is modest (strong published models reach ~0.64–0.68); this is an honest baseline, not a record. And ABNG remains experimental — I’m still iterating.

Prototype disclaimer

ABNG is a research-stage prototype, built on CJC-Lang, a research compiler that is itself pre-1.0. Both are under active development. The numbers here are a single, fully-reproducible snapshot from one experiment campaign — not a validated benchmark, not deployed, not peer-reviewed. Read them as honest exploratory engineering.

Where this sits in the ABNG series
  • ABNG: Treating Belief States as First-Class Citizens — the architecture.
  • Deterministic and Auditable Neural Systems — the audit chain + determinism contract.
  • Benchmarks Part I and Part II — the controlled benchmark deep-dive.
  • This post — the first time ABNG meets a real, messy, external dataset rather than a controlled benchmark.

1. Why a real dataset, and why this one

The earlier ABNG benchmark posts measured the architecture on controlled problems — synthetic PINNs, designed routing distributions, scripted drift scenarios. Those answer “does the mechanism work?” They do not answer “does it survive contact with real, messy data?”

So this post takes ABNG to a dataset I did not design: UCI Diabetes 130-US Hospitals for Years 1999–2008. It is a genuine, widely-cited clinical benchmark — de-identified records of 101,766 hospital encounters of diabetic patients across 130 US hospitals over ten years, introduced by Strack et al. (2014). The prediction task is the published one: will this patient be readmitted within 30 days?

It is a deliberately unforgiving choice on three axes at once:

  • It is genuinely complex. 50 heterogeneous columns: demographics, 23 medication columns, three ICD-9 diagnosis codes with 700+ distinct values each (very high cardinality), lab results, admission / discharge / source codes, visit counts. Missingness is heavy — weight is ~97% missing; payer_code and medical_specialty are largely missing.
  • It is class-imbalanced. Only ~11.2% of encounters are 30-day readmissions.
  • It is a hard target. Whether a patient is readmitted depends on post-discharge factors — social support, follow-up care, medication adherence — that simply are not in the data. That is irreducible noise. It is why strong published models on this task top out around AUC 0.64–0.68; nothing reaches 0.9 here, and a model that claimed to would be suspect.

That last point matters for reading everything below. On a dataset like this, a modest honest number is the correct outcome, and inflated numbers are the warning sign.

2. The evaluation protocol (the part that has to be right)

Before any result is worth reporting, the protocol has to be leakage-free. ABNG’s harness for this dataset does the following, and the discipline here is more important than any single metric:

  • The feature transform is fit on the training split only. Category vocabularies, one-hot widths, routing-feature selection — all learned from train rows, never from validation or test.
  • A 70 / 15 / 20-style stratified split into train / validation / test, stratified on the readmission label so all three splits share the ~11% positive rate.
  • Every tuning decision is made on validation, never on test. The decision threshold and the model hyperparameters are both selected by validation score; the test split is touched exactly once, for the final reported numbers.
  • Deterministic throughout. Fixed seed (42), deterministic SplitMix64 shuffles, deterministic reductions. Re-running reproduces every digit (see §7).

This is unglamorous, and it is the whole game. The most common way to get a flattering number on an imbalanced dataset is to let the test set leak into a tuning decision; the protocol above is designed so that cannot happen.

3. What changed to reach these numbers — honestly

Here is the question I most want to answer straight, because it would be easy to dress it up: what architectural changes did ABNG need to work at these numbers?

None. ABNG’s core architecture did not change.

The belief graph, the per-leaf Bayesian Linear Regression (BLR) heads with their closed-form conjugate updates, the SHA-256-hash-chained audit log, the deterministic execution contract — all of it is exactly as described in the earlier architecture post. Not one core data structure or update rule was modified to produce the results below.

What did change lives entirely around ABNG, not inside it:

Lever What it is Layer
More data 2,000 → 20,000-row stratified sub-sample Experiment design
Routing depth K_ROUTING tuned by validation sweep (see §5) ABNG configuration
BLR prior strength Prior precision tuned by the same sweep ABNG configuration
Decision threshold Tuned on validation, not left at 0.5 Harness post-processing
Probability calibration Post-hoc Platt scaling (see §4) Harness post-processing

K_ROUTING and the BLR prior are configuration knobs — dials ABNG already exposes, not new mechanisms. The threshold and the calibration are harness-side post-processing — they read ABNG’s outputs and never touch its state.

This is not a disappointing answer; it is the point. Determinism and auditability survived this entire experiment campaign precisely because the core was untouched. If “improving the metrics” had meant rewriting the BLR update or adding a new audit event kind, every replay guarantee and every hash-chain canary would have had to be re-validated. Instead, the 28 SHA-256 determinism canaries never moved, because nothing they cover was edited. The honest engineering lesson: reach for evaluation rigor and post-processing before you reach for architecture surgery.

4. The result — and what calibration fixed

4.1 Ranking: a real but modest signal

Held-out test AUC climbed 0.550 → 0.587 → 0.611: 2,000 rows with default settings, then 20,000 rows, then 20,000 rows with the validation-tuned configuration. That is real signal — the model orders patients by readmission risk better than chance — but it is modest, and it sits below the ~0.64–0.68 band where strong published models on this task live. I am reporting it as an honest baseline, not a result to headline.

4.2 Probabilities: the uncomfortable part, and the fix

AUC only measures ranking. The probabilistic metrics — Brier score and log-loss (NLL) — measure whether the predicted probabilities themselves are any good. And before calibration, they were not:

  • Raw Brier 0.100 — essentially tied with a do-nothing predictor that outputs the 11.2% base rate for everyone (its Brier is 0.099).
  • Raw NLL 0.391 — actually worse than that base-rate predictor (0.351).

The cause is structural: ABNG’s BLR head is linear regression. Linear regression has no link function — its output is unbounded and is not a calibrated probability. On 11%-imbalanced data its predictions cluster in a narrow band near the base rate, and the occasional out-of-range prediction, clamped into [0, 1], wrecks the log-loss.

The fix is post-hoc Platt calibration: fit a one-feature logistic — σ(a·raw + b) — on the validation predictions, then apply it to test. Two parameters, fit by Newton’s method, entirely harness-side.

The effect is exactly what the theory predicts:

Metric Raw Calibrated Base-rate baseline
AUC 0.611 0.611 0.500
Brier 0.100 0.098 0.099
Log-loss (NLL) 0.391 0.344 0.351
Calibration error (ECE) 0.031 0.010 0

AUC is bit-identical — calibration is a monotonic transform, so it cannot change the ranking, and it didn’t. What it did change is the probabilities: Brier and NLL both crossed from losing to the base-rate predictor to beating it, and calibration error fell to 0.010. The model’s probability outputs now genuinely carry information beyond “predict the average.”

A calibration honesty note

In an earlier draft of this analysis I described the raw ECE of 0.031 as “well-calibrated — a genuine strength.” That was wrong, and worth flagging. Raw ECE looked good only because the predictions were clustered into one or two histogram bins — Brier and NLL, which clustering cannot flatter, told the real story. After Platt calibration all three probabilistic metrics agree and all three beat the baseline. Calibration didn’t just improve numbers; it made the numbers consistent with each other. That internal consistency is what makes the result defensible.

4.3 The operating point

At the validation-tuned decision threshold, the tuned + calibrated model reaches balanced accuracy ≈ 0.58 and F1 ≈ 0.24 on the held-out test set.

A word on what is not reported as a headline: raw accuracy. On an 11%-positive dataset, a model that predicts “no readmission” for everyone scores ~89% accuracy with zero learning. Accuracy is the wrong yardstick here — it rewards the majority class — which is exactly why this post leads with AUC, balanced accuracy, and the probabilistic metrics instead.

5. The hyperparameter sweep — measure, don’t assume

The routing depth K_ROUTING controls how deep the belief tree’s routing partition goes — K routing features means 4^K leaves, each with its own BLR head. My prior assumption, baked into earlier defaults, was that more routing features (K = 4) would help.

A validation sweep over K ∈ {2, 3, 4} and three BLR prior strengths said otherwise:

K = 2 won, decisively. The mechanism is a data-starvation effect: with ~14,000 training rows, K = 2 gives 16 leaves (~875 rows each), while K = 4 gives 256 leaves (~55 rows each) — and each leaf trains its own BLR. Deeper routing buys “specialization” but starves every leaf of the data it needs. On this dataset, shallow wins. (Stronger prior regularization also helped, monotonically — unsurprising on a noisy target.)

This is a small but real instance of the discipline the whole project runs on: the assumption was wrong, the sweep was cheap, and the measurement settled it.

6. What ABNG features held up on real data

The metrics are modest, so I will not claim ABNG’s architecture produced a great model — it produced an honest, weak-but-real one. What I can report is which of ABNG’s properties survived contact with a messy real dataset:

  • Determinism held end-to-end. Two independent runs of the full pipeline — fit, train on real data, evaluate, calibrate — produced bit-identical metrics, despite a 3× spread in wall-clock time between them (§7). On most ML stacks, “re-run it” gives approximately the same number; here it gives the same bytes.
  • The audit chain stayed intact. Every training step appends to a SHA-256 hash chain. Across the full 101,766-row dataset and every experiment variant, the chain verified — auditability is not a toy-scale property that broke on real data.
  • The routing partition did real work. The K-sweep confirms the per-leaf structure is functional — different routing depths give measurably different models, and the partition is interpretable (you can read which leaf a patient routed to).
  • The BLR heads gave a probabilistic output to fix. Because each leaf is a Bayesian regressor rather than a bare classifier, there was a real-valued score for calibration to act on. A model with no probabilistic output would have had nothing for Platt scaling to repair.

In short: the architecture’s guarantees — determinism, auditability — held up perfectly; its predictive strength on this hard task is honestly modest and is the thing still being worked on.

7. Reproducibility

The result is deterministic. It was re-run from scratch to confirm:

Run 1:  finished in 30.0 s   AUC 0.6107  Brier 0.0980  NLL 0.3435  ECE 0.0101
Run 2:  finished in 90.9 s   AUC 0.6107  Brier 0.0980  NLL 0.3435  ECE 0.0101

The wall-clock differs 3× (machine contention); every metric digit is identical, and so is the fitted Platt (a, b) = (4.2946, −2.6183). That is the determinism contract working: the result is a pure function of (code, seed, data), decoupled from timing.

# From a clean checkout of the CJC-Lang repository:
git clone https://github.com/AdamEzzat1/CJC.git && cd CJC

# The Diabetes-130 experiment harness (dataset is untracked; fetch
# UCI dataset 296 into tests/data/diabetes_130/):
cargo test --test abng --release diabetes130_calibrated \
  -- --ignored --nocapture

Build fingerprint: Rust 1.91.1 (stable, LLVM 21.1.2), seed 42, 20,000-row stratified sub-sample, n_test = 3,002. Figures plotted with Python 3.11 / matplotlib 3.9.

8. Honest limitations

What this result is not
  • Not a record. AUC 0.611 is below the ~0.64–0.68 range of strong published Diabetes-130 models. It is an honest baseline.
  • Single seed. All numbers are seed 42. A multi-seed sweep to quantify variance is pending — until it lands, treat the third decimal as indicative, not pinned.
  • A 20,000-row sub-sample, not the full 101,766 rows. The full-dataset run is future work.
  • A linear model. ABNG’s BLR heads are linear; calibration fixes the probabilities but not the ranking ceiling. A nonlinear MLP-leaf-head variant is the next experiment — it may lift AUC, or may need a lot of tuning.
  • Not deployed, not validated. This is exploratory engineering.

9. Still experimenting

ABNG is, and is labelled, experimental — and this dataset is one stop on an ongoing campaign, not a finish line. Currently on the bench:

  • Nonlinear leaf heads. Swapping the linear BLR leaf for an MLP leaf head (a capability ABNG already has) to see whether the ranking ceiling moves — the lever calibration provably cannot touch.
  • The full 101,766-row run at the tuned configuration, for a headline number on all the data.
  • Multi-seed variance — running the pipeline across many seeds to report mean ± spread instead of a single point.

Each of these is the kind of measured, leakage-free, deterministic experiment this post is built on. If they move the numbers, a follow-up will say so honestly; if they don’t, it will say that too.

The honest summary

On a real, hard, imbalanced clinical dataset, ABNG produced a modest but genuine result — ranking meaningfully above chance, and, after calibration, probability estimates that beat the do-nothing baseline on every measure. It got there without any change to its core architecture: rigorous evaluation methodology, configuration tuning, and post-hoc calibration did the work, and the determinism and auditability guarantees held throughout because the core was never touched.

That is the result I can defend: not a strong model yet, but an honest one, fully reproducible, with a clear and unembellished account of how it was reached and where it falls short.

Note

ABNG lives at crates/cjc-abng/ in the CJC-Lang repository; the Diabetes-130 harness is in tests/abng/dataset_a_diabetes130.rs. This post is part of the ongoing ABNG series — see the architecture and determinism posts for the foundations the results above rest on.

item.title: Gibran v0.1.0: Natural-Language Analytics Without an LLM
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~25 min read
26NL patterns
25metric primitives
4engine adapters
764tests passing
TL;DR

Gibran is a governed analytics layer with a natural-language interface — but the NL is pattern templates, not an LLM. If a question can’t be resolved against your real catalog, gibran says “I don’t know.” It never invents. v0.1.0 ships 26 NL patterns, 25 metric primitives (including cohort retention, funnel, period-over-period, percentile), four database engine adapters (DuckDB / Postgres / Snowflake / BigQuery), and an optional local React UI. pip install gibran[ui], gibran init --sample, ask questions in English, get governance-correct SQL.

Why a Non-LLM NL Layer

Here’s the thing about LLM-based text-to-SQL: it works most of the time, fails some of the time, and you can’t always tell which mode you’re in. When a model hallucinates a column name that doesn’t exist, you usually find out at execution time — sometimes silently, when the model invents similar names that happen to compile but return wrong data.

For analytics, “wrong but plausible” is the worst possible failure mode. If the dashboard shows gross_revenue when the user asked for net_revenue, and both columns exist, no error is raised — the analyst just makes a decision on the wrong number. Models that are 95% accurate fail in exactly this way the other 5% of the time, and that 5% is statistically guaranteed to hit a load-bearing metric on a board deck eventually.

Gibran’s bet: trade NL breadth for hard correctness guarantees. The natural-language layer is a small set of regex patterns that extract slots, and every slot is resolved against your real AllowedSchema before becoming part of the SQL. If “biggest spenders” doesn’t match any registered metric or dimension, the question fails to parse — gibran says “I don’t know how to answer that” and exits.

The trade is real. Gibran can’t answer arbitrarily phrased questions. It can answer 26 well-defined question shapes, deterministically, with zero risk of inventing references.

Here’s the load-bearing line of code, in src/gibran/nl/patterns.py:

def top_n_by_metric(m: re.Match, schema: AllowedSchema) -> dict:
    n = int(m.group(1))
    dim_id = _resolve_dimension(m.group(2), schema)
    metric_id = _resolve_metric(m.group(3), schema)
    if not dim_id or not metric_id:
        raise NoMatch()       # <-- THE GUARANTEE
    return {
        "source": schema.source_id,
        "metrics": [metric_id],
        "dimensions": [{"id": dim_id}],
        "order_by": [{"key": metric_id, "direction": "desc"}],
        "limit": n,
    }

If _resolve_dimension("biggest spenders", schema) returns None because no such dimension exists, NoMatch is raised. The intent dict never includes the unresolved name. There is no code path that produces SQL referencing an unresolved slot. The guarantee is structural, not statistical.

The 26 NL Patterns

Each pattern is a regex + a builder function. The matcher walks them in order; the first one whose regex matches AND whose slots resolve wins. Here’s the full list with the question shape each one handles:

Pattern Example question
top_n_with_having top 5 region by revenue where order_count > 100
top_n_by_metric top 5 region by gross revenue
bottom_n_by_metric bottom 3 customers by churn rate
metric_by_type_keyword average order size
metric_by_grain revenue by month
metric_over_time revenue over time
metric_by_dim_and_grain revenue by region by week
metric_by_two_dims revenue by region and status
multi_metric revenue and order count by month
metric_by_dim revenue by region
metric_in_period revenue in 2026
metric_in_date_range revenue between 2026-01-01 and 2026-03-31
metric_this_period revenue this quarter
metric_last_n_period revenue last 30 days
count_with_condition count of paid orders
count_of_thing how many orders
metric_excluding_value revenue excluding refunded orders
metric_as_percent_of west revenue as percent of total revenue
metric_filter_compound revenue for west and paid
metric_where_between revenue where amount between 50 and 500
metric_where revenue where status = paid
metric_filtered_by_value revenue for west
metric_anomalies show me anomalies in revenue
metric_period_over_period revenue this month vs last month
metric_distribution distribution of order amount
single_metric gross revenue

Each one extracts typed slots, resolves them against your AllowedSchema, and produces a DSL intent that compiles to governance-correct SQL. The fall-through pattern single_metric is registered last, so more specific shapes always win first.

Pattern dispatch in practice

A user types “top 5 region by gross revenue” as the role analyst_west with attribute region=west. Here’s the sequence:

  1. Regex match: top_n_by_metric matches with groups (5, "region", "gross revenue").

  2. Slot resolution:

    • _resolve_dimension("region", schema) returns "orders.region" (case-insensitive match against display_name).
    • _resolve_metric("gross revenue", schema) returns "gross_revenue" (substring match against display_name).

  3. Intent built:

    {
  "source": "orders",
  "metrics": ["gross_revenue"],
  "dimensions": [{"id": "orders.region"}],
  "order_by": [{"key": "gross_revenue", "direction": "desc"}],
  "limit": 5
}

  4. DSL compile: produces SQL with metric expressions resolved from the catalog.

  5. Governance evaluate: identity has policy analyst_west_orders with row filter region = $attr.region. The filter is injected into the SQL string as an additional WHERE clause:

    SELECT "region" AS "orders.region",
       SUM(amount) FILTER(WHERE status = 'paid') AS "gross_revenue"
FROM "orders"
WHERE ("region" = 'west')           -- ← injected by governance
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY "gross_revenue" DESC
LIMIT 5

  6. Execute: returns one row, ('west', 100.00). The other regions are invisible to this identity — not because the application filters them out, but because the SQL itself never asked for them.

That sixth step is the load-bearing thing about gibran. The governance filter is woven into the compiled SQL, not bolted on at the application layer. There’s no code path that returns rows the policy doesn’t allow, because there’s no code path that queries for them.

What happens when the NL doesn’t match

If the analyst types “what’s the meaning of life?”, every pattern’s regex fails to match, OR the regex matches but the slots don’t resolve. The runner returns None. The CLI prints:

I don't know how to answer that.

And exits with code 4. No SQL is generated. No execution happens. No audit row is written for a query that didn’t exist. The contract is honest: gibran covers a fixed, documented set of shapes, deterministically. Outside that set, it refuses.

The 25 Metric Primitives

The NL layer can only ask for things the metric vocabulary supports. Here’s the full vocabulary:

Primitive What it does
count, count_distinct, count_distinct_approx Row counts (exact, distinct, HLL-approximate)
sum, avg, min, max Standard aggregates
ratio, expression Composed metrics: ratio of two metrics; arbitrary expression
percentile, median QUANTILE_CONT-based (configurable p; median is p=0.5 wrapper)
weighted_avg, mode Weighted average; statistical mode
variance, stddev_samp, stddev_pop Variance + sample/population standard deviation
first_value, last_value First / last value in an ordered series
rolling_window Window function: SUM(col) OVER (... RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '28 days' PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
period_over_period Compose two time-windowed metrics with delta / ratio / pct_change
cohort_retention Multi-CTE: cohort table → retention by cohort_age → matrix view
funnel Multi-CTE: per-step filter chain with conversion rates
multi_stage_filter Multi-CTE: entity-level filter chains (users who did X then Y)
cohort_filter Entity filter by sub-query intersection (users in cohort A AND cohort B)
anomaly_query Anomaly detection as a query (vs. as a check rule)

The advanced primitives are real multi-CTE SQL. Here’s what a cohort retention query compiles to:

WITH cohorts AS (
    SELECT customer_id,
           DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(order_date)) AS cohort_month
    FROM "orders"
    GROUP BY customer_id
),
events AS (
    SELECT o.customer_id,
           c.cohort_month,
           DATE_DIFF('month', c.cohort_month, o.order_date) AS months_since_cohort
    FROM "orders" o
    JOIN cohorts c USING (customer_id)
)
SELECT cohort_month,
       months_since_cohort,
       COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS retained
FROM events
GROUP BY cohort_month, months_since_cohort
ORDER BY cohort_month, months_since_cohort

That’s not a stored procedure or a Python aggregation — it’s a single SELECT that runs in DuckDB and returns the retention matrix as rows. The pattern matcher resolves the source + cohort dimension + retention dimension, the compiler emits the CTE chain, governance injects any row filter, and the result lands in the audit log like any other query.

Funnel is similar: each step is a CTE that filters the previous step’s entities by some condition, and the final SELECT computes step-by-step conversion rates. Four “shape primitives” (cohort_retention, funnel, multi_stage_filter, cohort_filter) get their own special-case branch in compile_intent because they produce whole-query CTE chains rather than the single-SELECT shape the simpler primitives use.

Governance: Woven In, Not Bolted On

Most metric layers treat access control as an HTTP-layer concern: an analyst hits an endpoint, the endpoint checks if their role can see the data, maybe rewrites the query. Gibran does it differently. The governance contract is:

No SQL leaves the compiler without the role’s row filter AND-ed into the WHERE clause, and no column appears in the SELECT list without an explicit grant from the role’s policy.

That contract is enforced by DefaultGovernance.evaluate(), which runs between compile and execute for every query — DSL or NL or raw SQL alike:

decision = governance.evaluate(
    identity,
    frozenset({source_id}),
    frozenset(requested_columns),
    metric_refs,
)
if not decision.allowed:
    return _record_denied(...)
rewritten = _inject_filter(sql, decision.injected_filter_sql) if decision.injected_filter_sql else sql

_inject_filter is a sqlglot AST rewrite — it parses the compiled SQL, adds the policy’s row filter as a top-level AND against the existing WHERE clause, and re-renders. The result is one SQL string that’s safe to execute.

The side-by-side demo

Same question, two roles. As analyst_west (region=west):

SELECT "region", SUM(amount) FILTER(WHERE status = 'paid') AS "gross_revenue"
FROM "orders"
WHERE ("region" = 'west')           -- governance row filter
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY "gross_revenue" DESC
LIMIT 5

Result: [('west', 100.00)] — only the analyst’s region.

As admin (break-glass role, no row filter):

SELECT "region", SUM(amount) FILTER(WHERE status = 'paid') AS "gross_revenue"
FROM "orders"
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY "gross_revenue" DESC
LIMIT 5

Result: [('north', 300.00), ('east', 200.00), ('west', 100.00)] — all three regions.

Same code, same NL prompt, same compiler. The SQL diverges based on the identity’s policy. There is no application code that filters out other regions for analyst_west; the SQL itself never asks for them. That’s the structural property: privilege escalation isn’t possible because there’s no boundary to escalate across.

Column-level governance

Policies can also forbid specific columns:

policies:
  - id: analyst_west_orders
    role: analyst_west
    source: orders
    default_column_mode: allow
    column_grants:
      customer_email: deny   # PII column the analyst can't read
    row_filter:
      op: eq
      column: region
      value:
        $attr: region

If analyst_west writes a DSL query referencing customer_email, the compile path fails before SQL is emitted with a clear deny reason. The query log records the attempt (with status=denied) so PII-access denials are auditable.

Audit log + redaction

Every query — allowed, denied, errored — writes a row to gibran_query_log:

query_id | user_id | role_id | nl_prompt | generated_sql | status | deny_reason | row_count | duration_ms | is_break_glass | created_at

Filter literals against PII or restricted columns are redacted before insertion:

-- The user ran:
SELECT order_id FROM orders WHERE customer_email = 'alice@example.com'

-- The audit log stores:
SELECT order_id FROM orders WHERE customer_email = '<redacted>'

This is a sqlglot walk over the SQL AST that finds binary comparisons (=, !=, LIKE, etc.), IN lists, and BETWEEN bounds where one side references a sensitive column, and replaces the literal value with '<redacted>'. The literal never reaches the audit table — so a future audit-log-reader (with whatever credentials) can never recover sensitive values from past queries.

Break-glass queries (admin role usage) are marked with is_break_glass=TRUE so elevated access is searchable. The UI’s Audit Log view renders break-glass rows with a red left border for visual review.

Four Engine Adapters

The compile path produces dialect-neutral SQL for the structural pieces (SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, window functions, CTEs). Where dialects diverge — interval syntax, DATE_DIFF shape, identifier quoting — gibran uses a per-dialect emitter registry.

Engine Identifier quote now() Interval syntax DATE_DIFF(seconds)
DuckDB "foo" now() INTERVAL '28 days' DATE_DIFF('second', a, b)
Postgres "foo" now() INTERVAL '28 days' CAST(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (b - a)) AS INTEGER)
Snowflake "foo" CURRENT_TIMESTAMP() INTERVAL '28 days' DATEDIFF('second', a, b)
BigQuery `foo` CURRENT_TIMESTAMP() INTERVAL 28 DAY TIMESTAMP_DIFF(b, a, SECOND)

The ExecutionEngine Protocol abstracts the connection, file-scan rendering, and SQL execution. Each engine has its own module under src/gibran/execution/engines/:

class ExecutionEngine(Protocol):
    @property
    def dialect(self) -> Dialect: ...
    def file_scan_sql(self, source_type: str, uri: str) -> str: ...
    def execute(self, sql: str, params: tuple | list = ()) -> None: ...
    def query(self, sql: str, params: tuple | list = ()) -> tuple[list[tuple], list[str]]: ...
    def fetchone(self, sql: str, params: tuple | list = ()) -> tuple | None: ...
    def commit(self) -> None: ...

Verified state

Engine Code Migrations Verified end-to-end?
DuckDB ✅ 11 migrations ✅
Postgres ✅ 11 translated (JSON → JSONB) ✅ via pgserver in CI — no Docker needed
Snowflake ✅ — Code only (paid account needed for E2E)
BigQuery ✅ — Code only (billing-enabled GCP project needed for E2E)

The Postgres integration tests auto-spin a real Postgres 16 binary via the pgserver Python package — every developer’s machine can run the full Postgres test suite without installing Docker or paying for a hosted instance.

Placeholder translation

Gibran’s internal SQL uses ? placeholders everywhere (DuckDB style). For Postgres + BigQuery, which use %s (pyformat), there’s a small state-machine translator that walks the SQL string and replaces ? outside string literals + comments + identifier quoting:

def translate_qmark_placeholders(sql: str) -> str:
    # Walks the SQL with a tiny state machine that tracks whether
    # we're inside a single-quoted string, double-quoted identifier,
    # or -- line comment. `?` only translates outside those scopes.
    # `%` is always doubled (psycopg parameter-substitution escape).

For Snowflake the connector supports paramstyle = 'qmark' natively, so no translation is needed.

Why this matters

The hard adoption blocker for gibran was “DuckDB-only” — most production analytics teams have Postgres or Snowflake or BigQuery. v0.1.0 ships the engine abstraction + the Postgres adapter fully verified. Snowflake and BigQuery have working adapter code but their integration tests are gated on credentials and skip by default; once you set GIBRAN_SNOWFLAKE_URL or GIBRAN_BIGQUERY_PROJECT, the same test suite runs against the real service.

The Local HTTP UI

Gibran’s CLI (gibran query, gibran ask, gibran check, gibran sync) is the primary surface. v0.1.0 adds a local HTTP UI as an optional install:

pip install 'gibran[ui]'
gibran init --sample
gibran sync
gibran ui            # opens http://127.0.0.1:<port> in your browser

The UI is a FastAPI app serving 16 endpoints + a bundled React SPA. The UI introduces no new governance logic — every endpoint is a thin wrapper around an existing core function, and identity comes from the same resolver the CLI uses.

Five views ship

  1. Query Workbench — Three-pane: NL prompt → compiled SQL preview → result table. An “Examples” panel below the input shows auto-generated questions (templated from your real metric + dimension names). One-click runs them. Cmd/Ctrl+Enter runs.

  2. Catalog Browser — Tree of sources → columns / metrics / dimensions, with PII / restricted / public sensitivity badges. Drill into any source to see exactly which columns the current role can read.

  3. Audit Log — Filterable view of gibran_query_log. Non-admin identities see only their own queries (the analyst’s “what have I asked recently”). Admin / break-glass identities see all queries. Break-glass rows are rendered with a red left border. Cursor pagination.

  4. Source Health — Per-source quality + freshness status with recent rule outcomes. Powered by gibran check.

  5. Policy Visualizer — Admin-only side-by-side: pick two roles, see exactly what AllowedSchema each one resolves to. The killer demo moment: analyst_west (with region=west attribute) next to admin (break-glass, no scoping). The column lists and accessible metrics differ visibly.

Identity flow

Request comes in
  -> middleware: extract identity from
       (a) X-Gibran-User / X-Gibran-Role / X-Gibran-Attrs headers (dev mode)
       (b) Authorization: Bearer <jwt> (prod mode, validated against JWKS)
  -> resolve to IdentityContext (user_id, role_id, attributes)
  -> if neither resolves: 401 Unauthorized
  -> endpoint reads via Depends(current_identity)
  -> NEVER reads identity from request body or path

Dev mode (headers, default) is fine for gibran ui on 127.0.0.1. For production, set GIBRAN_UI_AUTH_MODE=jwt and point GIBRAN_UI_JWT_JWKS_URL at your IdP. The CLI refuses to bind to a non-localhost host in dev mode — flipping to JWT mode is the only way to satisfy --host 0.0.0.0. Intentional friction.

What the UI is not

  • Not a dashboarding tool (no saved layouts, no scheduled refreshes, no sharing — those are V0.2+).
  • Not a charting tool (the Workbench renders tables; chart support is a follow-up).
  • Not a full ETL platform (gibran reads source data; pipeline orchestration is upstream).

Gibran is a query / governance / audit layer. The UI is what makes that layer usable by analysts who’d rather not write YAML.

How the Pieces Connect

┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐
│  NL prompt  │ -> │ Pattern matcher     │ -> │ Resolved intent  │
└─────────────┘    │ (6 → 26 patterns)   │    │ (dict, validated │
                   │  Slot resolution    │    │  against         │
                   │  against schema     │    │  AllowedSchema)  │
                   └─────────────────────┘    └────────┬─────────┘
                                                       v
                              ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                              │ DSL compiler                 │
                              │  + metric primitive registry │
                              │  + shape primitive registry  │
                              │  -> SQL string               │
                              └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                                             v
                              ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                              │ Governance.evaluate()        │
                              │  - role policy lookup        │
                              │  - column-grant check        │
                              │  - row-filter compilation    │
                              │  -> SQL with WHERE injected  │
                              └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                                             v
                              ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                              │ ExecutionEngine              │
                              │  (DuckDB / Postgres / SF /BQ)│
                              │  -> rows + columns           │
                              └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                                             v
                              ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                              │ Audit log: gibran_query_log  │
                              │  (redacted, break-glass flag,│
                              │   recorded for every attempt)│
                              └──────────────────────────────┘

The seams between these stages are deliberate. Pattern → intent is JSON. Intent → SQL is a pure compiler function (cacheable). Governance is a single chokepoint everything passes through. The execution engine is a Protocol with four implementations. The audit log writes happen for every query, even ones that fail.

What Ships

pip install gibran               # CLI + core + DuckDB
pip install 'gibran[ui]'         # + FastAPI + uvicorn + bundled React SPA
pip install 'gibran[postgres]'   # + psycopg
pip install 'gibran[snowflake]'  # + snowflake-connector-python
pip install 'gibran[bigquery]'   # + google-cloud-bigquery

The extras are additive — install what you need. The default install is DuckDB-only; the wheel is ~224 KB; no native dependencies beyond DuckDB itself.

Metric Value
Wheel size 224 KB
Source-only sdist size 567 KB
Tests passing 764
Frontend bundle size 52 KB gzipped (well under the 200 KB budget)
NL patterns 26
Metric primitives 25
Engine adapters 4 (DuckDB + Postgres verified end-to-end)
Migrations 11 (all 11 translated to Postgres)
External runtime deps (default install) DuckDB, pydantic, sqlglot, typer, pyjwt, pyyaml

What’s Not in v0.1.0

I want to be honest about the limitations:

  • Snowflake + BigQuery adapters are code only. The integration tests skip without credentials. The adapters compile, the unit tests pass, but I haven’t actually executed SELECT 1 against a paid Snowflake account or a billing-enabled GCP project.
  • The UI ships 5 of 11 planned views. Materialization Status, Cache Inspector, Approval Queue, Access Anomaly Alerts, and Onboarding Tour are documented follow-ups. The corresponding backend endpoints exist for 3 of those 5 already.
  • No Playwright E2E tests for the frontend. Vitest covers component logic (21 tests for the API client + IdentitySetup + QueryWorkbench), but full-browser smoke tests against a running server are deferred.
  • Multi-tenancy is V0.2. Single-tenant deployment for v0.1.0.
  • No charts. The Workbench renders result tables. Bar / line / scatter / pie are a 2-3 week add-on.
  • No dashboards (composed multi-query layouts with refresh).
  • Cross-process plan caching needs a shared store (Redis or similar). v0.1.0’s caches are per-process.

These are all known. The roadmap (ROADMAP.md) tracks the prioritization.

Try It

Install + spin up the sample:

pip install 'gibran[ui]'
gibran init --sample
gibran sync
gibran check
gibran ui

That last command opens your browser to a local FastAPI server. On the first launch, set identity to user=adam role=analyst_west attrs=region=west and click Continue. The Workbench appears with an Examples panel.

Click “top 5 region by gross revenue” → hit Run → watch the SQL preview show WHERE "region" = 'west' injected by governance. Result: one row, just the west region.

Then click change identity in the sidebar, switch to user=admin_user role=admin attrs= (blank), and run the same question. The SQL preview no longer has the WHERE clause. Result: all three regions.

That side-by-side, in the same UI, in 30 seconds, is the whole pitch.

# Or skip the UI and use the CLI directly:
gibran ask "top 5 region by gross revenue" \
  --source orders --role analyst_west --attr region=west

Where the Code Lives

  • GitHub: https://github.com/AdamEzzat1/gibran
  • PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/gibran/ (v0.1.0)
  • Roadmap: ROADMAP.md
  • Phase 4 UI handoff: PHASE_4_UI_HANDOFF.md
  • Deferred follow-ups: docs/DEFERRED_FOLLOWUPS.md
  • Demo script for the video: docs/DEMO_SCRIPT.md

MIT licensed. PRs welcome, especially if you have a real-world use case I haven’t thought about. The NL pattern list grows with user demand — if there’s a question shape that’s load-bearing for your team, file an issue.

Closing

The thesis is simple: there’s a class of analytics use case where “wrong but plausible” answers are catastrophic, and an LLM in the SQL emission path is structurally unable to give you the guarantee that wrong answers won’t happen. Pattern templates can give you that guarantee. They give up NL breadth in return.

Gibran v0.1.0 is the first release that’s complete enough to argue the thesis with code. 26 NL patterns covers about 70% of the question shapes a typical analyst types. The other 30% gets a clean “I don’t know” instead of a hallucination. The governance layer compiles into the SQL string so privilege escalation isn’t even a category of bug. The audit log is exhaustive and redacted. Four engine adapters mean it can plug into your existing warehouse instead of being a DuckDB toy.

If any of that sounds useful for what you’re building, install it and try it. If it’s missing a pattern or a primitive you actually need, the cost of adding one is roughly an afternoon. The code is short. The contract is honest.

item.title: ABNG: Treating Belief States as First-Class Citizens
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~35 min read
TL;DR

ABNG (Adaptive Belief Network Graph) is an experimental architecture that treats Bayesian belief as an architectural primitive, not a bolt-on. Each node in an adaptive tree carries its own Bayesian Linear Regression posterior, routing is a traceable walk through learned bytes, and the graph itself adapts to evidence via six structural actions (Grow / Split / Merge / Prune / Compress / Freeze). This article covers what the architecture is and why the design choices fit together. The follow-up articles cover the determinism story (Part 2) and the empirical evidence (Part 3).

Two prototype disclaimers up front

ABNG is a research-stage prototype, not a product. It has unit tests, property tests, fuzz coverage, a working CLI, and zero published benchmarks. It is also built on top of CJC-Lang, a research compiler that is itself still pre-1.0 — twenty-one Rust crates, roughly 96K lines of code, an actively evolving language definition. Anything ABNG inherits from the substrate inherits the substrate’s caveats. This piece is a design exploration meant for scrutiny, not a launch.

This is Part 1 of a three-part series
  • Part 1 (this article): the architecture itself.
  • Part 2 (Deterministic and Auditable Neural Systems): the SHA-256 audit chain, replay semantics, and the determinism contract.
  • Part 3 (Benchmarking Experimental Bayesian Graph Networks): the demo catalog, scaling benchmarks, and honest limitations.

Each part stands alone. Read in order for the full picture; read just Part 1 if you only care about the architectural shape; read Part 3 if you want the empirical evidence first.

Where the cracks show

There’s a recurring discomfort with most modern neural networks: they are confidently wrong without telling you, their decisions are hard to audit after the fact, and their compute graphs are fixed at design time even when the data isn’t. We have partial fixes for each — Bayesian layers, attention visualizations, mixture-of-experts, conformal wrappers — but they tend to be bolted onto architectures that did not begin with these properties in mind.

This article describes ABNG (Adaptive Belief Network Graph), an experimental architecture that tries the opposite: design from those constraints — posterior belief at every node, traceable routing, structural adaptation — and see what falls out.

If you came looking for a “transformer killer,” this isn’t that. It’s a sketch of a different shape of computation, with all the unfinished edges that implies.

Modern architectures produce accurate point estimates, but they share a small set of recurring discomforts.

Epistemic opacity. A model returns a number, and you have no principled way to know whether it is confident, guessing, or extrapolating beyond the data it saw at training time. Calibrated uncertainty exists as a layer you bolt on — temperature scaling, MC dropout, deep ensembles, conformal prediction — not as a property of the architecture itself.

Structural rigidity. Once you fix a transformer’s depth, width, and head count, the compute graph is set. Mixture-of-Experts adds conditional routing, but the expert population is still fixed. The model does not grow, prune, or specialize in response to evidence; the engineer does, by retraining.

Auditability and reproducibility tax. When a model trained over months produces a strange output, the lineage of that decision is essentially unrecoverable. And two “identical” training runs produce two different artifacts. Tolerable for research; painful for any system that must be replayed, audited, or proven correct. These two cracks are covered in depth in Part 2 — this article focuses on the architectural shape.

None of these are catastrophic alone. Together, they constrain where you can use modern ML responsibly, and they have driven a small zoo of partial fixes: Bayesian neural networks, probabilistic graphical models, neural state-space models, liquid networks, conformal prediction, active inference. Each addresses one or two pieces of the problem.

ABNG is an attempt to take a coherent run at several of them at once, by treating the model itself as an evolving belief state.

Belief states, used precisely

“Belief state” gets used loosely in ML. The term appears in at least four incompatible senses across the literature: the posterior over latent variables in a probabilistic graphical model, a sufficient statistic for prediction in control theory, a state estimator in a POMDP, and the Bayesian posterior of a model conditional on data. The shared word across these senses is genuinely misleading.

ABNG uses the fourth sense — specifically, the Bayesian posterior of a Bayesian Linear Regression head at each routed leaf. Each node in an adaptive graph carries a small per-node MLP that produces features, with a Bayesian Linear Regression (BLR) head on top — a Normal-Inverse-Gamma posterior over (weights, noise variance) that is updated in closed form as data arrives, with no variational machinery required. Routing uses the same Node4 / Node16 / Node48 / Node256 child layout as Adaptive Radix Trees (Leis, Kemper & Neumann, 2013), holding belief-graph children indexed by quantile-encoded feature bytes rather than database keys.

As observations flow through the graph:

  • Routing is a traceable walk through learned bytes, not an opaque attention pattern.
  • Each leaf separately reports epistemic uncertainty (the model’s uncertainty about its own parameters, from the BLR posterior) and aleatoric uncertainty (irreducible noise in the data, from the inverse-gamma component).
  • The graph itself can grow, split, merge, prune, compress, or freeze nodes — each action a deterministic function of per-node statistics under an installed DecisionPolicy.
